# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD). List any three functions of CSSD.**

**Model Answer (Board Pattern, 3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** CSSD is a centralized service department in a hospital that is responsible for the **reception, cleaning, assembly, packaging, sterilization, storage, and distribution** of sterile medical supplies, instruments, and equipment to various user departments such as OT, ICU, and wards, ensuring a uniform standard of sterilization and infection control.
>
> **Three Functions (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Sterilization** of all reusable medical devices and instruments using validated methods (steam, ETO, plasma).
> 2. **Distribution** of sterile supplies on demand to OT, ICU, wards, and OPD.
> 3. **Quality Assurance** through chemical and biological monitoring of every sterilization cycle.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the Spaulding Classification of medical devices. Why is HEPA filter used in OT?**

**Model Answer (Board Pattern, 3 Marks):**

> **Spaulding Classification (2 marks):** Earle Spaulding classified medical devices into three risk categories:
> - **Critical Devices** — Enter sterile tissue/vascular system → require sterilization (e.g., surgical implants, cardiac catheters).
> - **Semi-Critical Devices** — Contact mucous membranes/non-intact skin → require high-level disinfection (e.g., endoscopes, laryngoscopes).
> - **Non-Critical Devices** — Contact intact skin → require low-level disinfection (e.g., stethoscopes, BP cuffs).
>
> **HEPA in OT (1 mark):** HEPA (High Efficiency Particulate Air) filter removes **≥ 99.97%** of particles of size **0.3 µm** (Most Penetrating Particle Size), thereby reducing microbial load in OT air to **≤ 35 CFU/m³** and minimizing surgical site infections.

---

## Part B Question — Module 1 (14 Marks)
*Per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer ONE full question of 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a) Explain in detail the unidirectional workflow and zoning of a Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD). Discuss the role of the four physical zones in infection control. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The CSSD is designed on the principle of **unidirectional (one-way) flow**, ensuring that contaminated items move from "dirty" to "clean" areas without any backflow or cross-contamination. The physical layout is divided into **four pressure-controlled zones**.

**The Four CSSD Zones (4 marks):**

1. **Decontamination Zone (Dirty Zone):**
   - **Pressure:** Negative (–12.5 Pa) relative to surrounding areas.
   - **Function:** Receipt of soiled/contaminated instruments; pre-cleaning; manual washing; ultrasonic cleaning; washer-disinfector (93°C for 10 minutes).
   - **PPE:** Full PPE mandatory (gloves, gown, eye protection).

2. **Preparation Zone (Clean Zone):**
   - **Pressure:** Neutral (0 Pa).
   - **Function:** Inspection of instruments under illuminated magnifier; assembly of instrument sets; packaging in standard CSSD wraps/pouches/trays.
   - **Air:** Class 100,000 (ISO 8) cleanliness.

3. **Sterilization Zone (Hot Zone):**
   - **Pressure:** Positive (+12.5 Pa).
   - **Equipment:** Steam autoclaves, ETO sterilizers, H₂O₂ plasma units.
   - **Cooling:** Sterilized items cool here before moving to storage.

4. **Sterile Storage Zone (Cool Zone):**
   - **Pressure:** Positive (+12.5 Pa).
   - **Function:** Storage of sterile packs under controlled humidity (30–60%) and temperature (21–24°C); FIFO rotation; distribution cart dispatch.

**Unidirectional Flow Principle (1 mark):**
- Soiled cart enters **separately** from sterile cart exit.
- Separate doors, separate corridors, separate lifts.
- No instrument is allowed to retrace its path — this eliminates the **risk of recontamination**.

**Role in Infection Control (1 mark):**
The four-zone layout enforces **engineering separation of sterile and non-sterile streams**, supported by pressure cascades that physically prevent contaminated air from entering clean zones — the cornerstone of HAI (Hospital-Acquired Infection) prevention.

---

**(b) Discuss the engineering design requirements of a modern Operation Theatre suite. Include HVAC parameters, zoning, and laminar airflow in your answer. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern Operation Theatre (OT) is a **cleanroom-grade** surgical environment designed to minimize **Surgical Site Infections (SSI)** through controlled ventilation, temperature, humidity, pressure, and filtration. The design follows **NABH, ASHRAE 170**, and **ISHRAE** guidelines.

**OT HVAC Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Temperature | 21°C – 24°C |
| Relative Humidity | 40% – 60% |
| Air Changes/Hour | 20 – 25 fresh changes |
| HEPA Filter | H13/H14 (≥ 99.97% at 0.3 µm) |
| Pressure | +12.5 to +15 Pa positive |
| Airflow | Laminar (vertical preferred), velocity 0.3 – 0.5 m/s |
| Microbial Load | ≤ 35 CFU/m³ |
| Lux Level | 1000 general + 40,000+ surgical task |

**Zoning Concept (2 marks):**
The OT suite uses **four concentric zones** with progressive pressure cascade:
- **Outer (0 Pa)** → Reception, waiting
- **Clean (+5 Pa)** → Corridors, transfer
- **Aseptic (+10 Pa)** → Scrub, anesthesia induction
- **Protected (+15 Pa)** → Operation Theatre

**Laminar Airflow (1 mark):**
- **Type:** Vertical LAF (ceiling-mounted 3 m × 3 m grid) preferred over horizontal.
- **Principle:** Parallel, non-turbulent, unidirectional air streams sweep contaminants away from the surgical field.
- **HEPA Position:** HEPA filter is the **terminal filter** at the discharge point (in the ceiling), not at the AHU — to avoid recontamination in ductwork.

**Conclusion (Bonus):**
A properly designed modular OT reduces SSI by up to **70%** and improves surgical outcomes, making HVAC design a **patient safety imperative**, not merely a comfort feature.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a Smart Hospital. List at least four IoT applications used in a Smart Hospital. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A **Smart Hospital** integrates **IoT, AI, Big Data, and Building Management Systems (BMS)** to deliver data-driven, patient-centric, and energy-efficient care. Its architecture is layered, similar to the OSI model in networking.

**Layered Architecture (4 marks) — refer to SECTION 4.3 diagram:**

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor Layer):** IoT sensors (temperature, humidity, CO₂, RFID tags, biometric monitors, smart beds) collect real-time data from the physical environment and patients.

2. **Network Layer (Connectivity Layer):** Wi-Fi 6, 5G, LoRaWAN, and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) provide seamless, low-latency communication between sensors and gateways.

3. **Edge Layer (Processing Layer):** Local gateways perform preliminary AI inference, reducing cloud load and latency. Critical alerts (e.g., patient deterioration) are processed in real-time.

4. **Platform Layer (Cloud & Analytics):** AWS/Azure cloud servers host AI/ML models for predictive analytics, hospital digital twin, and big-data HIS integration.

5. **Application Layer (End-User Layer):** HIS, EHR, mobile apps, and 24×7 command center dashboards deliver actionable insights to clinicians, administrators, and patients.

**Four IoT Applications in Smart Hospital (2 marks):**

1. **RFID Asset Tracking** — Real-time tracking of infusion pumps, wheelchairs, ventilators; reduces equipment loss by 30%.
2. **Smart HVAC & Energy Management** — Sensor-driven AHU control; cuts OT energy consumption by 18%.
3. **Predictive Patient Monitoring** — Wearables + AI predict cardiac arrest 6–12 hours before occurrence.
4. **Automated CSSD Tracking** — RFID-tagged instrument sets track every step from decontamination to OT, ensuring 100% sterilization traceability.

**Conclusion (Bonus):**
Smart hospitals represent the convergence of **engineering, IT, and medicine** — biomedical engineers play a pivotal role in their design and commissioning.

---

**(b) Compare the four major sterilization methods used in CSSD. Which method is preferred for heat-sensitive and moisture-sensitive devices? Justify your answer. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Steam (Autoclave) | ETO | H₂O₂ Plasma | Dry Heat |
|-----------|------------------|------|--------------|----------|
| **Temperature** | 121–134°C | 37–63°C | 45–55°C | 160–180°C |
| **Pressure** | 15–30 psi | Vacuum | Vacuum | Atmospheric |
| **Cycle Time** | 15–60 min | 2–6 h + aeration | 45–75 min | 30 min – 2 h |
| **Mechanism** | Protein coagulation | Alkylation | Free radical | Oxidation |
| **Heat Sensitive?** | No | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Moisture Sensitive?** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Toxic Residue** | None | EtO residue | Only H₂O | None |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Application** | Linen, metal, glass | Plastics, electronics | Endoscopes, cameras | Powders, oils |

**Preferred Method for Heat + Moisture Sensitive Devices (2 marks):**
- **H₂O₂ Plasma Sterilization** is preferred.
- **Justification:** It operates at low temperature (45–55°C) AND uses dry plasma (no moisture), so it is safe for **laparoscopic instruments, flexible endoscopes, fiber-optic cameras, and electronic devices**. Moreover, it leaves **no toxic residue** (only water and oxygen), making it environmentally and occupationally safer than ETO.

**Disadvantage of ETO (1 mark):**
- EtO is a **known carcinogen**, requires long aeration (8–12 hours), and has longer cycles. H₂O₂ plasma is now the gold standard for modern CSSDs (e.g., Sterrad® by Johnson & Johnson).

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Module (Common Board Pitfalls):**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing disinfection with sterilization** — disinfection ≠ sterilization. Sterilization eliminates ALL life; disinfection only pathogens.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting the unidirectional principle** — saying "soiled items come in, sterile items go out" without explaining *one-way flow + pressure cascade* costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. ❌ **Wrong HEPA efficiency figure** — saying "99.99%" instead of "99.97% @ 0.3 µm" — this is a **favourite 1-mark trick question** by KTU examiners.
> 4. ❌ **Not mentioning positive pressure value** — saying "OT has positive pressure" without **+12.5 Pa** is incomplete. Always quote the numerical value.
> 5. ❌ **Spaulding Classification errors** — swapping "critical" and "semi-critical" categories loses full marks for the question.
> 6. ❌ **Smart Hospital = "Wi-Fi hospital"** — IoT, AI, and digital twin are core. Students who write only "computerized billing" miss the depth expected.
> 7. ❌ **No units in formulas** — ACH, F-value, ΔP must have units (h⁻¹, min, Pa) or board deducts 0.5 marks per wrong/missing unit.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — High-Density Revision List**

### CSSD Essentials
- ✅ **Full form:** Central Sterile Supply Department
- ✅ **Core principle:** Unidirectional flow (soiled → clean → sterile → dispatch)
- ✅ **Four zones:** Decontamination (–12.5 Pa) → Preparation (0 Pa) → Sterilization (+12.5 Pa) → Sterile Storage (+12.5 Pa)
- ✅ **Spaulding:** Critical = Sterilize; Semi-critical = High-level disinfect; Non-critical = Low-level disinfect
- ✅ **Biological indicators:** *Bacillus stearothermophilus* (steam), *B. atrophaeus* (EtO/Dry heat)
- ✅ **SAL:** Probability ≤ 10⁻⁶ of one viable microbe per million items

### Sterilization Methods
- ✅ **Steam (Autoclave):** 121°C @ 15 psi × 15 min OR 134°C @ 30 psi × 3 min — most common, cheapest
- ✅ **ETO:** Low temp (37–63°C), 2–6 h cycle, toxic — for heat-sensitive items
- ✅ **H₂O₂ Plasma:** 45–55°C, 45–75 min, no toxic residue — **gold standard for modern CSSD**
- ✅ **Dry Heat:** 160°C × 2 h — for powders, oils, sharp instruments
- ✅ **F-value formula:** $F = 10^{(T-121)/10} \times t$ — measures steam lethality

### Operation Theatre Essentials
- ✅ **Temperature:** 21–24°C | **Humidity:** 40–60% | **ACH:** 20–25
- ✅ **HEPA:** ≥ 99.97% @ 0.3 µm (H13/H14 grade)
- ✅ **Pressure:** +12.5 to +15 Pa positive
- ✅ **Microbial Load:** ≤ 35 CFU/m³
- ✅ **Zoning (4 zones):** Outer (0) → Clean (+5) → Aseptic (+10) → Protected (+15) Pa
- ✅ **Laminar Flow:** Vertical preferred, 3 m × 3 m ceiling, velocity 0.3–0.5 m/s
- ✅ **ACH formula:** $ACH = (Q \times 60) / V$

### Smart Hospital Essentials
- ✅ **Five Pillars:** Infrastructure, Clinical Care, Operations, Patient Experience, Energy & Safety
- ✅ **Six-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Network → Edge → Platform → Application → Service
- ✅ **Key IoT Apps:** RFID tracking, smart HVAC, predictive vitals, automated CSSD, digital twin
- ✅ **Standards:** NABH Digital Hospital (Level 6), JCI, ASHRAE 170, ISHRAE Healthcare Handbook

> **Final Board Tip:** Always end your 14-mark answer with a **1-line conclusion** linking the topic to patient safety — examiners award 0.5–1 mark for holistic perspective.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Power Systems in Hospitals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT501 - HOSPITAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Power Systems in Hospitals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Power Systems in Hospitals** refers to the integrated network of high-tension (HT) intake, step-down transformation, low-tension (LT) distribution, protective switchgear, earthing arrangements, and standby generation that delivers safe, continuous, and quality electrical energy to life-support, diagnostic, surgical, lighting, HVAC, and ancillary loads within a healthcare facility. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, this system must comply with the **National Electrical Code (NEC)**, **IS 732**, **IS 3043** (earthing), and the **IEC 60364-7-710** standard for medical locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A hospital is classified as a "**Group 1 / Group 2 Medical Location**" depending on the type of medical procedures performed. The power system architecture, earthing topology, and backup requirements vary based on this classification.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Medical Locations (IEC 60364-7-710)

| Group | Application | Power Reliability Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Group 0** | General outpatient areas, waiting rooms, non-clinical zones | Standard supply |
| **Group 1** | Wards, general treatment rooms, ECG, ultrasound | Normal + Changeover within **15 seconds** |
| **Group 2** | Operation theatres (OT), ICU, CCU, cath lab, MRI, CT scan | Normal + **SEC (Safety Extra-Low Voltage) + UPS** with changeover ≤ **0.5 seconds** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital as a Living Organism**
> 
> Think of a hospital's electrical system as the **circulatory system of a human body**:
> - **HT incoming line** = the *aorta* (large high-pressure artery carrying the bulk supply)
> - **Transformers** = the *heart* (pumps and steps down pressure for the body to use)
> - **LT distribution panels (DBs)** = *capillaries* (deliver energy to organs/loads)
> - **DG Sets + UPS + Batteries** = *stored oxygen in the lungs* (rescue when the main supply fails)
> - **Earthing pits** = *kidneys* (filter out electrical faults/leakages safely to the ground)
> - **Isolation Transformers (IT System)** = *placenta* (creates a sterile, isolated environment for the most delicate "patient" — the OT table)
> 
> If the heart (transformer) stops, the body (hospital) collapses — hence the layered redundancy.

## 1.3 Standard Voltage Levels Used in Hospitals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Tier Reference (India)**
> - **HT (High Tension):** **11 kV** or **22 kV** (utility supply standard in Kerala KSEB)
> - **LT (Low Tension):** **415 V (3-phase)** and **240 V (1-phase)**
> - **SEC (Safety Extra-Low Voltage):** ≤ **25 V AC** or ≤ **60 V DC** for patient-connected circuits
> - **FELV (Functional Extra-Low Voltage):** ≤ **12 V** for invasive surgical equipment
> - **ELV (Extra-Low Voltage):** ≤ **50 V AC / 120 V DC**

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

- **Reliability Index:** SAIDI (System Average Interruption Duration Index) target = **< 2 minutes/year** for OTs
- **Availability:** **99.9999 %** ("Six Nines" = 31.5 seconds downtime/year) for Group 2 locations
- **Power Quality:** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) ≤ **5 %** for voltage, ≤ **8 %** for current
- **Voltage Regulation:** ± **5 %** steady state, ± **10 %** momentary

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Hospital Power System

A modern hospital power system is structured in **five hierarchical tiers**:

### Tier 1 — HT Intake & Substation
- Utility supply at **11 kV** (Kerala) or **22 kV** enters via HT cable.
- **HT Switchgear** (VCB — Vacuum Circuit Breaker) protects the line.
- **Step-down Transformer** (typically **500 kVA to 2500 kVA** for a 100–500 bed hospital) reduces **11 kV → 433 V**.
- Transformer vector group: **Dyn11** is preferred (delta primary, star secondary with neutral) for harmonic suppression.

### Tier 2 — LT Main Switchboard (PCC — Power Control Centre)
- Receives **415 V, 3-phase, 4-wire** from the transformer.
- Houses **ACBs (Air Circuit Breakers)** for main incomer, bus coupler, and outgoing feeders.
- Contains **APFC (Automatic Power Factor Correction)** panels.

### Tier 3 — Sub-distribution Boards (SDBs) & Final Distribution Boards (DBs)
- Floor-wise, wing-wise, and OT-cluster-wise distribution.
- Contains **MCCBs (Moulded Case Circuit Breakers)** and **MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers)**.

### Tier 4 — Critical Branch (Life Safety + Equipment)
- Fed through **UPS** (online double-conversion, **0 ms transfer**).
- Critical loads: ventilators, monitors, OT lights, anaesthesia workstations.

### Tier 5 — Standby Power
- **DG Sets** (Diesel Generator) — capacity sized at **1.25× to 1.5×** connected critical load.
- **UPS with battery bank** for instantaneous ride-through until DG starts.
- **Captive power / Solar PV** for grid resilience.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **How the system operates under normal conditions:**
> 1. KSEB supplies **11 kV** via overhead/underground HT cable to the hospital substation.
> 2. HT VCB closes; transformer steps down to **415 V**.
> 3. ACB at LT panel closes; APFC panel maintains power factor at **0.95–0.98 lagging**.
> 4. LT feeders route power through rising mains (bus ducts) to floor distribution boards.
> 5. Critical loads are fed from UPS (battery float charged); non-critical from raw LT bus.
> 
> **What happens during power failure:**
> 1. Within **< 4 ms**, UPS senses the blackout and switches to battery (online UPS = true **0 ms**).
> 2. DG set receives start signal; reaches rated **voltage and frequency** within **10–15 seconds**.
> 3. ATS (Automatic Transfer Switch) connects DG to essential bus.
> 4. Non-essential loads are shed via load-shedding contactors to size the DG appropriately.

## 2.3 Earthing Systems Used in Hospitals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earthing is the single most safety-critical subsystem. KTU examiners frequently test this.**

| Earthing System | Symbol | Description | Use in Hospitals |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TN-S** | TN-S | Separate Protective Earth (PE) and Neutral (N) from transformer to load | **Most common** for non-medical areas |
| **TN-C-S** (PME) | TN-C-S | Combined PEN upstream, separated into PE+N downstream | **NOT recommended** for OT/ICU |
| **TT** | TT | Earth electrode at consumer; no shared neutral | Used in some auxiliary buildings |
| **IT** | IT | Isolated Terra — no direct connection between live conductors and earth | **Mandatory for Group 2 OT circuits** as per IEC 60364-7-710 |

### 2.3.1 Medical IT System (Isolated Power System)

- An **isolation transformer (1:1, typically 3.15 kVA to 10 kVA)** with **230 V single-phase secondary** is used.
- The secondary is **completely isolated from earth** (Insulation Monitoring Device — IMD continuously measures leakage).
- A **first fault does not trip** the breaker; the IMD only alarms. This is critical during surgery — a single leakage must not black out an OT.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are KTU 2024 exam favourites.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-phase power | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | W | Load calculation |
| 2 | Single-phase power | $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | W | Small load calc |
| 3 | Energy consumed | $E = P \times t$ | kWh | Billing / DG fuel |
| 4 | Voltage drop (3-φ) | $V_d = \sqrt{3} \times I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | V | Cable sizing |
| 5 | Cable current rating | $I_z = k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3 \times I_n$ | A | Derating factors |
| 6 | DG set sizing | $DG_{kVA} = \dfrac{\Sigma P_{critical}}{\cos\phi \times \eta_{alt}}$ | kVA | Backup planning |
| 7 | UPS sizing | $UPS_{kVA} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{PF \times SafetyFactor}$ | kVA | Critical power |
| 8 | Battery backup time | $t_b = \dfrac{Ah \times V_{batt}}{P_{load}}$ | hours | UPS autonomy |
| 9 | PF correction capacitor | $Q_c = P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | kVAR | APFC |
| 10 | Earth resistance (rod) | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | Earthing design |
| 11 | Earth resistance (plate) | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ (for hemispherical) | Ω | Plate earthing |
| 12 | Specific energy of battery | $E_{batt} = V \times Ah$ | Wh | Battery sizing |
| 13 | Star-delta conversion | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \times I_{ph}$, $V_L = \sqrt{3} \times V_{ph}$ | — | Transformer |
| 14 | Fault level (3-φ SC) | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \times Z_s}$ | kA | Breaker selection |
| 15 | Transformer efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{iron} + P_{cu}}$ | % | Energy audit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Symbols:**
> - $V_L, I_L$ = Line voltage, line current
> - $R, X$ = Resistance, reactance of cable (Ω/km)
> - $L$ = cable length (km)
> - $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m)
> - $d$ = rod diameter (m)
> - $k_1, k_2, k_3$ = derating factors (temperature, grouping, soil)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital commissioning teams** use formula #6 to size DG sets — under-sized DG is a leading cause of OT blackouts.
- **Biomedical engineers** use formula #8 to size UPS batteries for ventilators during inter-DG changeover.
- **Facility managers** use formula #9 to compute APFC capacitor ratings, saving lakhs in EB (electricity board) penalty charges.
- **Electrical safety auditors** use formula #10 to verify earth resistance ≤ **1 Ω** for OT/ICU (KSEB regulation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DG Set Sizing for a Hospital

**Problem Context:** A 200-bed hospital has the following critical loads to be backed by a DG set. Calculate the DG capacity in kVA.

| Load | Quantity | Rating (W) each | Total (W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| OT Light (LED) | 4 | 200 | 800 |
| Ventilator | 8 | 150 | 1,200 |
| Patient Monitor | 15 | 80 | 1,200 |
| OT Table | 2 | 500 | 1,000 |
| Suction Pump | 4 | 100 | 400 |
| Anaesthesia Workstation | 2 | 300 | 600 |
| MRI Chiller | 1 | 7,500 | 7,500 |
| Lift (1 OT lift) | 1 | 5,000 | 5,000 |
| Corridor + Exit Lighting | — | — | 1,500 |
| **Total Critical Load** | | | **19,200 W** |

### Step 1 — Apply Diversity Factor
Realistic operating diversity for a hospital critical load = **0.7**

$$P_{design} = 19200 \times 0.7 = 13440 \text{ W}$$

*Logic: Not all ventilators run simultaneously; some OTs are idle. A 0.7 factor accounts for practical loading.*

### Step 2 — Add Future Expansion Margin
Standard practice: add **20 %** margin

$$P_{final} = 13440 \times 1.20 = 16128 \text{ W}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kVA Using Power Factor and Alternator Efficiency
Assume alternator PF = 0.8 and efficiency = 0.85

$$DG_{kVA} = \frac{P_{final}}{PF \times \eta}$$

$$DG_{kVA} = \frac{16128}{0.8 \times 0.85} = \frac{16128}{0.68}$$

$$DG_{kVA} = 23,717.6 \text{ VA} \approx 23.72 \text{ kVA}$$

### Step 4 — Select the Next Standard DG Rating
Standard DG sizes: 25, 30, 40, 50, 62.5, 82.5, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 320, 380, 500 kVA

$$\boxed{DG_{selected} = 25 \text{ kVA}}$$

*Engineering Note: Always oversize by at least one standard rating to avoid overload trips. A 25 kVA DG is the minimum acceptable.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — UPS Battery Sizing for OT Cluster

**Problem Context:** An OT cluster draws **2.4 kW** (3 OTs, each 800 W critical equipment). The DG takes **15 seconds** to come online. The UPS must support the load for at least **15 minutes** (with margin).

**Specifications:**
- UPS output: **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase**
- UPS efficiency: **0.92**
- Battery type: **12 V SMF VRLA, 100 Ah**
- DC bus voltage: choose a 16-battery string of 12 V = **192 V DC** (typical for 3 kVA UPS)

### Step 1 — Convert Load to DC Demand
The UPS AC output of 2.4 kW must be supported by DC bus:

$$P_{DC} = \frac{P_{AC}}{\eta_{UPS}} = \frac{2400}{0.92} = 2608.7 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — DC Current Required

$$I_{DC} = \frac{P_{DC}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{2608.7}{192} = 13.59 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Required Ampere-Hours for 15 Minutes

$$Ah_{req} = I_{DC} \times t_{hrs} = 13.59 \times \frac{15}{60} = 13.59 \times 0.25$$

$$Ah_{req} = 3.397 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Derating Factors
- Temperature derating (ambient 25 °C): 1.0
- Ageing factor: **1.25** (batteries lose capacity over time)
- Inverter ripple factor: **1.10**
- Design margin: **1.20**

$$Ah_{final} = 3.397 \times 1.25 \times 1.10 \times 1.20$$

$$Ah_{final} = 3.397 \times 1.65 = 5.605 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 5 — Select Battery Size
Standard available: 7 Ah, 12 Ah, 26 Ah, 42 Ah, 65 Ah, 100 Ah SMF batteries.

$$\boxed{\text{Select } 12 \text{ V}, 12 \text{ Ah battery} \times 16 \text{ nos. in series (192 V DC bus)}}$$

*Engineering Note: A 100 Ah battery would give ~2.5 hours backup, but is unnecessarily large and costly. 12 Ah is the optimal economic-engineering choice.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Factor Correction Capacitor Sizing

**Problem Context:** A hospital has an average load of **800 kW** at an existing power factor of **0.75 lagging**. The electricity board (KSEB) penalises below **0.95** PF. Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank required to bring PF to 0.95.

### Step 1 — Identify Initial and Target Angles
For PF₁ = 0.75: $\cos\phi_1 = 0.75 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41°$

For PF₂ = 0.95: $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \Rightarrow \phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$

### Step 2 — Apply Capacitor Rating Formula

$$Q_c = P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$

$$\tan(41.41°) = 0.8819$$

$$\tan(18.19°) = 0.3287$$

$$Q_c = 800 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287) = 800 \times 0.5532$$

$$Q_c = 442.56 \text{ kVAR}$$

### Step 3 — Verify by Drawing the Power Triangle

$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{800}{0.75} = 1066.67 \text{ kVA}$$

$$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2} = \frac{800}{0.95} = 842.10 \text{ kVA}$$

$$Q_1 = S_1 \times \sin\phi_1 = 1066.67 \times 0.6614 = 705.5 \text{ kVAR}$$

$$Q_2 = S_2 \times \sin\phi_2 = 842.10 \times 0.3122 = 262.9 \text{ kVAR}$$

$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 705.5 - 262.9 = 442.6 \text{ kVAR} \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Select Standard Capacitor Bank

$$\boxed{\text{Select APFC panel of 450 kVAR (in steps of 25/50 kVAR)}}$$

*Engineering Note: The capacitor bank should be an **APFC** (Automatic) type with 6–12 steps. This compensates dynamically as load varies throughout the day.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Earth Resistance for Hospital Plate Earthing

**Problem Context:** Design a plate earthing system for an OT block. Soil resistivity $\rho = 80 \text{ Ω·m}$. Plate size: 60 cm × 60 cm × 6.3 mm GI plate.

### Step 1 — Compute Equivalent Hemisphere Radius
A square plate of side $a = 0.6 \text{ m}$ approximates a hemisphere of radius:

$$r = \frac{a}{2} = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hemispherical Electrode Formula

$$R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi r} = \frac{80}{2 \times \pi \times 0.3} = \frac{80}{1.885}$$

$$R_e = 42.44 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Multiple Electrodes in Parallel
A single plate gives 42 Ω, which is too high (target ≤ 1 Ω for OT). Use **multiple plates in parallel** with **spaced salt + charcoal layer**.

Using 3 parallel plates spaced 3 m apart with **30 kg salt + 30 kg charcoal per pit**:

$$R_{combined} = \frac{R_{single}}{n} = \frac{42.44}{3} = 14.15 \text{ Ω}$$

With salt/charcoal moisture retention, effective reduction factor = **0.1**:

$$R_{effective} = 14.15 \times 0.1 = 1.415 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 4 — Add Chemical Earthing Enhancement
Use **chemical backfill compound (Marconite / Bentonite)** that maintains moisture for years:

$$R_{final} = 1.415 \times 0.7 = 0.99 \text{ Ω} \approx 1 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R_{earth} \leq 1 \text{ Ω (acceptable for OT per IEC)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choice of Earthing System

| Criteria | TN-S | TN-C-S (PME) | TT | IT (Medical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Medium | High |
| **First-fault continuity** | Trips | Trips | Trips | **No trip (Alarm only)** |
| **OT suitability** | Acceptable | **Not allowed** | Not preferred | **Mandatory for Group 2** |
| **Earth fault loop impedance** | Low | Low | High | Very high (safe) |
| **Need for RCD/ELCB** | Yes | Yes | **Mandatory** | IMD replaces RCD |
| **Maintenance complexity** | Low | Low | Medium | **High (specialist)** |
| **Patient microshock risk** | Medium | **High** | Medium | **Lowest** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Insight:** When asked "Why IT system in OT?" the answer must include — "IT system prevents nuisance tripping on first earth fault, which could cause OT blackout mid-surgery. The IMD alerts staff to fix the fault at convenience without endangering the patient."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain the Medical IT (Isolated Terra) system used in operation theatres. Why is it preferred over TN-S in Group 2 medical locations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Medical IT system** uses an **isolation transformer (1:1 ratio, typically 5–10 kVA)** to create a locally isolated 230 V single-phase supply that has **no direct connection to earth**. An **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** continuously measures the insulation resistance between the live conductors and earth. (1 mark)

In a **TN-S system**, the moment an earth fault occurs (e.g., a wet surgical tool touches the patient's body), the **RCD/ELCB trips**, cutting off power to the OT, which can be **fatal mid-surgery**. (1 mark)

In the **IT system**, the **first earth fault does not trip** the breaker. The IMD only raises an alarm at the nurse station, allowing surgery to continue safely while the fault is rectified afterwards. This makes the IT system **mandatory for Group 2 medical locations** as per IEC 60364-7-710. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any six essential loads that must be backed by a DG set in a hospital.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Operation Theatre lighting and equipment** (OT lights, anaesthesia workstation, electrosurgical unit)
2. **Life-support equipment** (ventilators, defibrillators, patient monitors, infusion pumps)
3. **ICU / CCU / NICU critical equipment**
4. **Medical imaging equipment** (CT scan, MRI, X-ray, ultrasound)
5. **Lift for OT block** (stretcher lift)
6. **Fire-fighting and sprinkler pumps** + Emergency corridor and exit lighting + CSSD (Central Sterile Supply Department) + Blood bank refrigerators

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A 250-bed district hospital has the following critical electrical loads that must be backed by a DG set:**

| Load | Quantity | Rating each (W) | Total (W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ventilator | 12 | 200 | 2,400 |
| Patient Monitor | 30 | 90 | 2,700 |
| OT Light | 6 | 250 | 1,500 |
| Anaesthesia Workstation | 3 | 400 | 1,200 |
| Suction Pump | 6 | 150 | 900 |
| CT Scanner (auxiliaries) | 1 | 3,000 | 3,000 |
| MRI Chiller | 1 | 8,000 | 8,000 |
| Lift (OT) | 1 | 7,500 | 7,500 |
| Corridor + Exit Lighting | — | — | 2,000 |

**(a)** Calculate the **DG set rating in kVA** using a diversity factor of 0.7, future expansion margin of 25 %, power factor 0.8, and alternator efficiency 0.88. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a **neat sketch** the **single-line diagram of the LT side** of a hospital substation showing transformer, ACB, bus coupler, DG, ATS, and UPS. Also list the **changeover time requirements** for Group 1 and Group 2 medical locations. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Total Connected Critical Load**

$$\Sigma P_{connected} = 2400 + 2700 + 1500 + 1200 + 900 + 3000 + 8000 + 7500 + 2000$$

$$\Sigma P_{connected} = 29{,}200 \text{ W}$$

*Logic: Sum of all individual critical load wattages listed in the table. (1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Apply Diversity Factor (0.7)**

$$P_{diversified} = 29200 \times 0.7 = 20{,}440 \text{ W}$$

*Logic: Not all loads operate simultaneously — ventilators and CT scanner are rarely at full load together. (1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Apply Future Expansion Margin (25 %)**

$$P_{final} = 20440 \times 1.25 = 25{,}550 \text{ W}$$

*Logic: Hospital loads grow with new equipment, additional beds, and stricter regulations. (1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Convert to kVA Using PF and Efficiency**

$$DG_{kVA} = \frac{P_{final}}{PF \times \eta} = \frac{25550}{0.8 \times 0.88} = \frac{25550}{0.704}$$

$$DG_{kVA} = 36{,}292.6 \text{ VA} \approx 36.29 \text{ kVA}$$

*Logic: P = S × cosφ × η, hence S = P / (cosφ × η). (2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Select Standard DG Rating**

Standard ratings: 40, 50, 62.5, 82.5, 100 kVA. Pick next higher size.

$$\boxed{DG_{selected} = 40 \text{ kVA}}$$

*Logic: Standardisation allows for spare parts availability and ensures DG operates at 75–80 % loading (best efficiency point). (2 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Single-Line Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[11 kV KSEB] → [HT VCB] → [Transformer 11kV/433V] → [LT PCC]
                                                           |
                                                    [ACB 1]   [ACB 2]
                                                           ↕
                                                    [Bus Coupler]
                                                     |              |
                                              [Essential Bus]   [Non-Essential Bus]
                                                     |              |
                                                 [UPS 40kVA]   [Floor DBs]
                                                     |
                                              [Battery Bank]
                                                     |
                                               [DG 40kVA] ← [ATS]
```

**Changeover Time Requirements (4 marks):**

- **Group 1 (General wards, treatment rooms):** Mains failure → backup power available within **≤ 15 seconds**. This allows DG to start and stabilise. UPS not mandatory for all Group 1 loads. (1.5 marks)
- **Group 2 (OT, ICU, CCU, Cath lab):** Mains failure → **uninterrupted supply within ≤ 0.5 seconds** (i.e., 500 ms). This is achieved by **online double-conversion UPS with 0 ms transfer**, while the DG starts in parallel for longer outages. (1.5 marks)
- **Life-safety systems** (fire pumps, emergency lights): Within **≤ 15 seconds** as per NBC 2016. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **earthing system** used in a hospital. Compare **TN-S, TT, and IT systems** with respect to suitability for OTs. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hospital has a connected load of **1200 kW** with an existing **power factor of 0.72 lagging**. Calculate the **capacitor bank rating** (in kVAR) to improve the power factor to **0.96**. Also estimate the **annual savings** in electricity charges if the tariff is ₹7.50/kVAh. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Hospital Earthing System (3 marks):**

A hospital requires **multiple dedicated earth pits** with the following typical resistance values:
- **Body earth (equipment earth):** ≤ **1 Ω** for OT/ICU, ≤ **2 Ω** for general areas
- **Neutral earth (system earth):** ≤ **1 Ω** at transformer star point
- **Lightning protection earth:** ≤ **5 Ω**, separate from body earth
- **Electronic equipment earth (computer/server):** ≤ **0.5 Ω** with separate pit

Earth pits use **GI plate (60 cm × 60 cm × 6 mm)** or **copper-bonded rod (3 m long, 17 mm dia)** with **salt + charcoal + chemical backfill** (Marconite or Bentonite) to retain moisture. Earth conductors are colour-coded: **green for protective earth, green-yellow striped for protective + neutral, black for neutral**. Earth resistance must be tested **annually** using a **fall-of-potential method** with earth tester.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | TN-S | TT | IT (Medical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earth fault behaviour** | Earth fault current → trips protective device | Earth fault current limited by earth electrode resistance | **First fault: alarm only, no trip** |
| **Patient microshock risk** | Medium (requires RCD) | Medium-High | **Very low** |
| **OT applicability** | Acceptable for non-critical | Not preferred | **Mandatory for Group 2** |
| **RCD/ELCB required** | Yes, 30 mA | Yes, 30 mA | **IMD replaces RCD** |
| **Cost & complexity** | Low | Medium | High |

*Conclusion:* The **IT system is mandatory for Group 2 medical locations** because any earth fault trip during surgery can be life-threatening. The IMD continues monitoring while surgery proceeds uninterrupted.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Find Initial and Target Angles**

$$\cos\phi_1 = 0.72 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.95°$$

$$\cos\phi_2 = 0.96 \Rightarrow \phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.96) = 16.26°$$

*Logic: Use standard inverse-cosine values from the trigonometry table. (1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute Tangs**

$$\tan(43.95°) = 0.963$$

$$\tan(16.26°) = 0.292$$

*Logic: Tangent of the phase angle is the ratio of reactive to active power. (1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Apply Capacitor Formula**

$$Q_c = P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 1200 \times (0.963 - 0.292)$$

$$Q_c = 1200 \times 0.671 = 805.2 \text{ kVAR}$$

*Logic: This reactive power must be supplied locally to offset the inductive reactive demand. (1.5 marks)*

**Step 4 — Select Standard Capacitor Bank**

$$\boxed{Q_{c,selected} = 825 \text{ kVAR (next standard size)}}$$

*Logic: Standard capacitor banks come in 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 500, 600, 800, 1000 kVAR ratings. (1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Compute kVA Reduction and Annual Savings**

Before PF correction:

$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{1200}{0.72} = 1666.67 \text{ kVA}$$

After PF correction:

$$S_2 = \frac{1200}{0.96} = 1250 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\Delta S = 1666.67 - 1250 = 416.67 \text{ kVA reduction in apparent power}$$

*Logic: This is the demand reduction the utility sees. (1 mark)*

Assuming **24 hours × 365 days = 8760 hours/year** with 70 % load utilisation:

$$E_{saved} = 416.67 \times 0.70 \times 8760 = 2{,}554{,}613 \text{ kVAh/year} \approx 25.55 \text{ lakh kVAh}$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 2554613 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ }1{,}95{,}59{,}600 \approx \text{₹ 1.96 crore/year}$$

*Logic: Even after 30 % losses, net savings are substantial — typically the APFC pays back in 6–10 months. (1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.25× safety factor** in DG sizing — valuator deducts **1–2 marks** if no margin is included.
> 2. **Mixing up Earth Resistance formulae** — Rod earthing ≠ Plate earthing. The hemispherical formula applies only to plate; rod uses the logarithmic expression.
> 3. **Writing "TN-S" for OT** — TN-S is acceptable in NON-OT areas. For OT Group 2, **IT system is mandatory**. Mentioning TN-S for OT will cost **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not specifying "first fault, no trip"** in IT system answer — this is the *core differentiator* and the examiner expects this exact phrase.
> 5. **Confusing cos φ with sin φ** in the capacitor formula. The standard formula is $\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2$, not the sine difference.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in final answer (kVA vs W, kVAR vs kVA). Always quote units.
> 7. **Not drawing the IMD** in the IT system diagram — without IMD, the diagram is incomplete and loses marks.
> 8. **Confusing "changeover" with "ride-through"** — UPS provides 0 ms; DG provides ≤ 15 s. Both serve different purposes.
> 
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** Always end a numerical answer with a **standardised selected value** (e.g., "DG = 40 kVA", "Battery = 12 V × 16 nos × 12 Ah") — valuators give bonus marks for practical engineering judgement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

### 5.4.1 Definitions to Memorise
- ✅ **Medical IT system:** Isolated 230 V system with no direct earth; first fault alarms only
- ✅ **Group 0 / 1 / 2 medical locations** — based on type of medical procedure
- ✅ **TN-S, TN-C-S, TT, IT** — four earthing topologies; **IT mandatory for OT Group 2**
- ✅ **SEC / FELV / ELV** — three safety voltage tiers below 50 V
- ✅ **APFC** — Automatic Power Factor Correction using capacitor banks

### 5.4.2 Numerical Formulas (Memorise the Standard Form)
- ✅ Three-phase power: $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$
- ✅ DG sizing: $DG_{kVA} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{PF \cdot \eta} \times \text{Diversity} \times \text{Margin}$
- ✅ UPS autonomy: $t_b = \dfrac{Ah \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{load}}$
- ✅ Capacitor: $Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$
- ✅ Earth resistance (rod): $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$
- ✅ Voltage drop (3-φ): $V_d = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$

### 5.4.3 Standards to Remember
- ✅ **IEC 60364-7-710** — Medical electrical installations
- ✅ **IS 732** — Code of practice for electrical wiring
- ✅ **IS 3043** — Code of practice for earthing
- ✅ **IS 12360** — Voltage bands for AC transmission
- ✅ **NBC 2016 (Part 4)** — Fire and life safety in hospitals
- ✅ **NFPA 99 (USA)** — Healthcare facilities code (referenced globally)

### 5.4.4 Changeover Time Hierarchy
- ✅ **Group 0:** Standard (no requirement)
- ✅ **Group 1:** ≤ 15 seconds
- ✅ **Group 2:** ≤ 0.5 seconds (UPS) + ≤ 15 seconds (DG)

### 5.4.5 Earth Resistance Targets
- ✅ **OT / ICU / Cath Lab:** ≤ **1 Ω** (single earth pit, multiple electrodes in parallel)
- ✅ **General hospital areas:** ≤ **2 Ω**
- ✅ **Lightning protection:** ≤ **5 Ω** (separate pit)
- ✅ **Server / IT earth:** ≤ **0.5 Ω** (dedicated pit)

### 5.4.6 Key Engineering Judgements
- ✅ DG capacity = **1.25× to 1.5×** of diversified critical load
- ✅ UPS battery = **sized for 15 min** minimum (covers DG start time + margin)
- ✅ PF correction target = **0.95–0.98** to avoid KSEB penalty
- ✅ Transformer vector group = **Dyn11** for harmonic suppression
- ✅ Bus duct / rising main current density = **1.0 A / sqmm** for copper, **0.8 A / sqmm** for aluminium

### 5.4.7 One-Liner Exam Punchlines
- ✅ "**IT system: first fault = alarm, not trip**" — the mantra of OT safety
- ✅ "**Hospital = mission-critical infrastructure, not a commercial building**"
- ✅ "**Earthing saves lives; poor earthing costs lives**"
- ✅ "**APFC pays for itself in 6–10 months**"
- ✅ "**Diversity factor of 0.7 is industry-standard for hospital critical loads**"

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hospital Substation Design

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Hospital Substation** is an integrated electrical power receiving, transforming, switching, and distributing station specifically engineered to deliver Grade-A reliability, redundancy (N+1 or 2N configuration), and ultra-low downtime power to life-support, diagnostic, and therapeutic medical equipment, conforming to **IEC 60364-7-710** and the **National Electrical Code (NEC) Article 517** for healthcare facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A hospital substation is NOT merely a utility receiving station. It must ensure **99.99% availability** with **automatic changeover in < 10 seconds** (per IS 10500 / IEC 60364-7-710) for critical life-safety circuits.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a hospital substation as the **"heart" of a hospital's electrical system**. Just as the human heart has redundant valves, backup mechanisms (autonomic nervous system), and supplies purified blood to vital organs without interruption, a hospital substation:
- Receives raw **"oxygenated" power** from the utility grid
- Filters it through **"lungs"** (transformers and filters)
- Has a **"pacemaker backup"** (DG sets + UPS)
- Delivers **"purified, oxygen-rich blood"** (clean, stable power) to critical **"organs"** (OT, ICU, CCU, NICU)

---

## 1.2 Stabilized Power Supply (SPS) Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A **Stabilized Power Supply** is a power conditioning system that maintains output voltage within a tight tolerance band (typically **±1% to ±5%**) despite variations in input voltage, load current, and environmental conditions, using servo-mechanical, ferro-resonant, or solid-state (IGBT/SCR) regulation topologies.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **water tap with a pressure regulator** — even if the municipal water pressure (input) fluctuates wildly from 1 bar to 5 bar, the regulator ensures a **constant 2 bar flow** to your shower. A stabilizer does exactly the same for electricity — it "regulates the pressure" (voltage) for sensitive medical equipment.

---

## 1.3 Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS) Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A **UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply)** is a power electronics device that provides **instantaneous, gap-free, conditioned backup power** to critical loads during mains failure by drawing energy from a stored DC source (batteries, supercapacitors, or flywheels) via a static inverter, achieving **transfer time < 4 ms** (online double-conversion type).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A UPS is the **"emergency parachute"** of an electrical system. When the main "aircraft engine" (utility grid) fails, the parachute (UPS battery + inverter) opens in **less than 4 milliseconds** — so fast that the "passenger" (patient on ventilator) never notices the fall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three UPS Topologies (KTU Board Favorite):**
> 1. **Offline / Passive Standby** – Transfer time 5–10 ms; cheapest.
> 2. **Line Interactive** – Transfer time 2–4 ms; uses AVR.
> 3. **Online Double Conversion** – Transfer time **0 ms** (true online); gold standard for ICU/OT.

---

## 1.4 Protective Systems

| Protective Device | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-Voltage Protector** | Limits surges > 270 V (single-phase) | "Pressure relief valve" |
| **Over-Current Protector (MCB/MCCB/ELCB)** | Trips on sustained overcurrent / earth leakage | "Circuit breaker fuse box" |
| **Circuit Breaker (ACB/VACB/SF₆)** | Interrupts fault currents up to **100 kA** | "Emergency stop button" |
| **Surge Protector (SPD Type 1/2/3)** | Diverts lightning/transient surges to earth | "Lightning rod for electronics" |
| **EMI Filter** | Suppresses conducted/radiated electromagnetic interference | "Noise-cancelling headphones" |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Protective devices are the **"immune system"** of a hospital's electrical network. Just as white blood cells attack viruses, surge protectors attack voltage spikes, MCBs attack overcurrents, and EMI filters attack electrical "noise" — keeping the "body" (hospital) healthy.

---

## 1.5 Hospital Electrical Safety & ISO Standards

**Formal Definition:**
**Hospital Electrical Safety** is the systematic application of engineering controls, design practices, device standards, and procedural safeguards — codified under **IEC 60601-1** (Medical Electrical Equipment safety), **ISO 13485** (Quality Management for Medical Devices), **ISO 14971** (Risk Management), **IEC 60364-7-710** (Medical Locations), and **NFPA 99 / NFPA 70** (Healthcare Facilities Code) — to protect **patients**, **operators**, and **the environment** from electrical hazards including **microshock (≤ 10 mA)** and **macroshock**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Hospital electrical safety standards are the **"rulebook and safety harness"** of a mountain climber. The harness (insulation, grounding, isolation transformers) keeps the climber (patient) alive even if they slip, while the rulebook (ISO/IEC standards) ensures every guide (engineer) follows the same proven protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patient Safety vs Equipment Safety** — these are TWO different paradigms:
> - **Equipment Safety** → protect the machine
> - **Patient Safety** → protect the **electrically susceptible patient** who may have direct electrical connections to the heart (e.g., catheter, pacemaker lead) where currents as low as **10 µA** can induce **ventricular fibrillation**.

---

## 1.6 Radiation Room Safety (Planning & Installation)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Radiation Room** (X-ray, CT, Linear Accelerator, PET, or Brachytherapy suite) is a specially engineered, **shielded, controlled-access** enclosure designed per **ICRP (International Commission on Radiological Protection)**, **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India)**, **NCRP Report No. 147**, and **IEC 60601-2-44** for CT / IEC 60601-2-1 for accelerators — to contain **ionizing radiation** within prescribed **dose limits** for occupational, public, and embryo-fetal exposure.

**Key Safety Metrics:**
- **Occupational dose limit:** **20 mSv/year** averaged over 5 years (ICRP 103)
- **Public dose limit:** **1 mSv/year**
- **Embryo/fetal dose limit:** **1 mSv** during declared pregnancy
- **Shielding design goal:** **ALARA** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A radiation room is like a **bank vault for X-rays**. The walls are made of **lead/concrete/borated polyethylene** (the vault door), the door interlocks are the **security cameras**, and the radiation monitor is the **security guard** — ensuring the dangerous "currency" (radiation) never leaks out to harm the public.

---

## 1.7 Patient Safety in Hospital Engineering

**Formal Definition:**
**Patient Safety** in the context of hospital engineering encompasses the **electrical, mechanical, fire, environmental, and procedural** design of healthcare facilities — including **isolated power systems (IPS)**, **medical IT systems**, **equipotential bonding**, and **leakage current limits** — to prevent adverse events, hospital-acquired infections, equipment malfunction, and physical injury.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Principle:** *Primum non nocere* — "First, do no harm." Patient safety is the **highest-order design constraint** that overrides cost, aesthetics, and convenience.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hospital Substation Design – Operational Logic

A hospital substation follows a **layered, redundant architecture**. Below is the structured logic broken into engineering steps:

### Step 1: Load Assessment
- Compute **connected load**, **diversity factor**, and **maximum demand**.
- Categorize loads into **Life-Safety (Type 1)**, **Critical (Type 2)**, and **General (Type 3)** per **NFPA 99 / IS 10500**.

### Step 2: Source Selection
- **Utility (EB) + DG (Standby) + UPS (Critical)** — minimum 3-tier redundancy.
- For 1000-bed hospitals: **2N configuration** (two independent substations).

### Step 3: Voltage Transformation
- **HT side:** **11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV**
- **LT side:** **433 V / 415 V, 3-phase, 4-wire**
- **Phase-to-neutral for medical IT:** **230 V ± 5%**

### Step 4: Short Circuit & Coordination Study
- Calculate **short circuit MVA** at each bus.
- Coordinate **relay settings** to ensure **discrimination** (selectivity).

### Step 5: Earthing System Design
- **TN-S system** for general areas.
- **IT system (Isolated Terra)** for **Group 1 & 2 medical locations** (per IEC 60364-7-710).

### Step 6: Power Quality & Conditioning
- Harmonic filters (Active / Passive)
- Surge Protection Devices
- EMI/RFI filters
- Voltage Stabilizers (Servo / Static)

---

## 2.2 UPS System Sizing Logic

### Step-by-Step Design:
1. **List all critical loads** (ventilators, monitors, infusion pumps, OT lights, PACS servers).
2. **Sum their power (VA)**, add **20% margin**.
3. **Determine backup time** — typically **15 min** (for OT) to **30 min** (for ICU) per **IEC 60364-7-710** and **NFPA 99**.
4. **Calculate battery bank capacity** in Ah.
5. **Size the inverter** for **125% of peak load**.

### Battery Sizing Formula (Lead-Acid):
$$C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{load} \times t_{backup}}{V_{DC} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{batt}}$$

Where:
- $C_{Ah}$ = required battery capacity (Ah)
- $P_{load}$ = total load (W)
- $t_{backup}$ = backup time (h)
- $V_{DC}$ = DC bus voltage (typically 12 V / 48 V / 120 V / 240 V / 384 V)
- $\eta_{inv}$ = inverter efficiency (~0.92–0.96)
- $\eta_{batt}$ = battery efficiency (~0.80–0.85)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** For a 10 kVA UPS at 0.8 PF running 30 minutes on 192 V DC battery (96 cells × 2 V) with 90% inverter efficiency and 85% battery efficiency — expect a battery bank of **~150 Ah** (C10 rating).

---

## 2.3 Protective System Design

### 2.3.1 Over-Current Protection Setting
For an MCB/MCCB protecting a feeder:
$$I_{set} \geq 1.25 \times I_{FL}$$
$$I_{set} \leq I_{cable\ ampacity}$$

Where:
- $I_{FL}$ = Full-load current of the load
- $I_{set}$ = protection trip setting
- Factor **1.25** accounts for **harmonic currents** and **temporary overloads**.

### 2.3.2 Short Circuit Current Calculation
At any point in the LV network:
$$I_{sc} = \frac{kVA_{transformer} \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times Z_{total}(\%)}$$

For impedance-based calculation:
$$I_{sc} = \frac{V_{ph}}{Z_{eq}}$$

### 2.3.3 Voltage Drop in Feeders
$$\Delta V\% = \frac{I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times 100}{V_{LL}^{2}}$$

**Permissible limits (per IS 732 / IEC 60364):**
- **Motors:** 5% (during starting) and 3% (running)
- **Lighting:** 3% (from transformer to farthest point)
- **Medical IT (IPS):** **5%** max

### 2.3.4 Surge Protection (SPD) Selection
$$U_p \leq 0.8 \times U_{ch}$$

Where:
- $U_p$ = voltage protection level of SPD (kV)
- $U_{ch}$ = rated impulse voltage of equipment (e.g., 6 kV for Category IV, 4 kV for Cat III, 2.5 kV for Cat II, 1.5 kV for Cat I)

### 2.3.5 Touch Voltage & Earth Resistance
**Permissible touch voltage** (medical):
- AC: **≤ 25 V** (normal), **≤ 6 V** (medical IT in wet areas)
- Earth electrode resistance: **≤ 1 Ω** for hospital substations (vs 5 Ω for industrial)

---

## 2.4 Radiation Room Shielding – Core Physics

### 2.4.1 Half-Value Layer (HVL) and Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)
The HVL is the thickness of a material that reduces radiation intensity by 50%:
$$I = I_0 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = I_0 \times e^{-\mu x}$$

Where:
- $n = x / HVL$
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $x$ = shield thickness (cm)

The **TVL** (Tenth-Value Layer) ≈ **3.32 × HVL**

### 2.4.2 Required Shielding Thickness
$$x = HVL \times \log_2 \left(\frac{P \times U}{P_{lim} \times d^{2}}\right)$$

Where:
- $P$ = workload (mA·min/week or Gy·m²/week)
- $U$ = use factor (fraction of time beam points toward barrier)
- $P_{lim}$ = weekly design dose limit (Sv/week)
- $d$ = distance from source to barrier (m)

### 2.4.3 Concrete & Lead Equivalence
- **1 mm Lead ≈ 6 cm Concrete** (for 100 kVp diagnostic X-rays)
- **Density of standard shielding concrete:** **2.35 g/cm³**
- **Density of lead:** **11.34 g/cm³**

---

## 2.5 Medical Safety Leakage Current Limits (per IEC 60601-1, 3rd Ed.)

| Leakage Type | Class I (Type B) | Class I (Type BF) | Class I (Type CF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earth Leakage (NC)** | 5 mA | 5 mA | 5 mA |
| **Enclosure Leakage (NC)** | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA |
| **Patient Leakage (NC)** | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | **0.01 mA (10 µA)** |
| **Patient Leakage (SFC)** | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | **0.05 mA (50 µA)** |

> **NC** = Normal Condition | **SFC** = Single Fault Condition
> **Type B** = Body (general) | **Type BF** = Body Floating (skin contact) | **Type CF** = **Cardiac Floating** (direct heart contact — most stringent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Fact:** **Type CF equipment** (e.g., intracardiac catheters, pacemaker programmers, intra-aortic balloon pumps) requires **Patient Leakage ≤ 10 µA** — **10× more stringent** than Type B. This is the basis for the **Isolated Power System (IPS)** mandated in **Group 2 Medical Locations** (cath labs, OT, ICU, CCU).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full-load current (3φ) | $I_{FL} = \frac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$ | A | kVA = transformer/load rating |
| 2 | Full-load current (1φ) | $I_{FL} = \frac{kVA \times 1000}{V_{LN}}$ | A | For single-phase circuits |
| 3 | Battery capacity | $C_{Ah} = \frac{P \times t}{V_{DC} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{batt}}$ | Ah | UPS design |
| 4 | Short circuit current | $I_{sc} = \frac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL} \times Z_{\%}/100}$ | kA | At transformer terminals |
| 5 | Voltage drop (%) | $\Delta V\% = \frac{I L (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times 100}{V_{LL}^{2}}$ | % | For LV feeders |
| 6 | Earth resistance | $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | Rod electrode (per Dwight's) |
| 7 | HVL attenuation | $I = I_0 \cdot 2^{-x/HVL}$ | — | For narrow beam |
| 8 | TVL from HVL | $TVL = 3.32 \times HVL$ | cm | Equivalent shielding |
| 9 | Barrier thickness | $x = TVL \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{P \cdot U}{P_{lim} \cdot d^{2}}\right)$ | cm | NCRP 147 methodology |
| 10 | SPD voltage level | $U_p \leq 0.8 \cdot U_{ch}$ | kV | Per IEC 60364-4-44 |
| 11 | Apparent power | $S = \sqrt{3} V_{LL} I$ | VA | 3-phase |
| 12 | Real power | $P = \sqrt{3} V_{LL} I \cos\phi$ | W | 3-phase |
| 13 | Generator sizing | $kVA_{DG} \geq 1.25 \times kVA_{max\ load}$ | kVA | For motor starting surges |
| 14 | Earth fault loop | $Z_s \leq \frac{U_0}{I_a}$ | Ω | Per BS 7671 / IS 732 |
| 15 | Charging time (battery) | $t = \frac{C \times V}{I_{chg} \times \eta}$ | h | Float/boost charging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Equations:**
> - (1)–(3): UPS specification & procurement for ICUs, OTs, data centers.
> - (4)–(5): Protection coordination & cable sizing.
> - (6), (14): Earthing design for safety.
> - (7)–(9): AERB-approved radiation room design.
> - (10): Surge protection layout for MRI, CT, and linear accelerators.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: UPS Battery Bank Sizing (Hospital OT)

### Problem Statement:
A 500-bed hospital Operating Theatre complex has critical loads totalling **18 kW** at **0.85 power factor**. The UPS (online double-conversion) is rated **230 V, 1-phase output** with a **192 V DC bus** (96 cells × 2 V). Required backup = **30 minutes**. Inverter efficiency = **0.93**, battery efficiency = **0.82**, derating factor = **1.25**. Calculate the required battery bank capacity in Ah.

### Given Data:
- $P_{load} = 18\ \text{kW} = 18{,}000\ \text{W}$
- $PF = 0.85$ → $S = P / PF = 18{,}000 / 0.85 = 21{,}176\ \text{VA} \approx 21.2\ \text{kVA}$
- $t_{backup} = 30\ \text{min} = 0.5\ \text{h}$
- $V_{DC} = 192\ \text{V}$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.93$
- $\eta_{batt} = 0.82$
- Derating factor $= 1.25$

### Step 1: Compute Energy Demand at DC Bus
The inverter must deliver 18 kW AC. After inverter losses, the DC bus must supply:
$$P_{DC} = \frac{P_{AC}}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{18{,}000}{0.93}$$

$$P_{DC} = 19{,}354.84\ \text{W}$$

### Step 2: Account for Battery Discharge Losses
The batteries themselves have internal resistance and discharge losses, so the actual energy that must be drawn from the cells is:
$$P_{cells} = \frac{P_{DC}}{\eta_{batt}} = \frac{19{,}354.84}{0.82}$$

$$P_{cells} = 23{,}603.46\ \text{W}$$

### Step 3: Apply Derating Factor
Hospitals apply a **safety derating** of 1.25 to compensate for battery aging, temperature, and depth-of-discharge (DoD) limits (lead-acid typically limited to **80% DoD**):
$$P_{design} = P_{cells} \times 1.25 = 23{,}603.46 \times 1.25$$

$$P_{design} = 29{,}504.33\ \text{W}$$

### Step 4: Compute Battery Capacity
For $t = 0.5\ \text{h}$ and $V_{DC} = 192\ \text{V}$:
$$C_{Ah} = \frac{P_{design} \times t}{V_{DC}} = \frac{29{,}504.33 \times 0.5}{192}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \frac{14{,}752.16}{192} = 76.83\ \text{Ah}$$

### Step 5: Round Up to Standard Rating
Standard battery sizes are 12 V / 100 Ah, 150 Ah, 200 Ah. Therefore:
$$\boxed{C_{selected} = 100\ \text{Ah}\ (\text{at C10 rate})}$$

This implies a battery configuration of **16 × 12 V 100 Ah** batteries (96 cells / 6 cells per 12 V block = 16 blocks in series).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** Real-world OT installations typically use **150 Ah or 200 Ah** banks to also cover future load additions and to allow DoD < 80%, extending battery life to **5–7 years**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Short Circuit Current at a Hospital Substation Bus

### Problem:
A **1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V** transformer with **5.5% impedance** feeds the main LT panel of a hospital. Compute the **symmetrical short circuit current (rms)** at the LT bus.

### Given:
- $S_{tr} = 1000\ \text{kVA}$
- $V_{LL} = 433\ \text{V}$
- $Z_{\%} = 5.5$

### Step 1: Full-Load Current of Transformer
$$I_{FL} = \frac{kVA \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{750.05}$$

$$I_{FL} = 1333.3\ \text{A}$$

### Step 2: Symmetrical Short Circuit Current
At the LV terminals, the short circuit current is:
$$I_{sc} = \frac{I_{FL}}{Z_{\%}/100} = \frac{1333.3}{0.055}$$

$$I_{sc} = 24{,}242.4\ \text{A} \approx 24.24\ \text{kA}$$

### Step 3: Peak (Asymmetrical) Short Circuit Current
For LV systems, peak current ≈ **2.55 × I_sc** (per IEC 60865, accounting for DC decay):
$$I_{peak} = 2.55 \times 24.24 = 61.8\ \text{kA}$$

### Step 4: Selection of Circuit Breaker
The ACB at the LT bus must have a **breaking capacity (Icu) ≥ 25 kA** (or 36 kA with 25% margin), and a **short-time withstand (Icw) for 1 second ≥ I_peak/√2 ≈ 43.7 kA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** Whenever a transformer impedance is given, ALWAYS compute I_FL first, then divide by Z_%/100 to get I_sc. Markers explicitly look for the **√3** factor — do not omit it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Radiation Room Barrier Thickness (Lead)

### Problem:
A 3D conformal radiotherapy **linear accelerator (LINAC)** operates at **6 MV** photons with a workload of **1000 Gy·m²/week**. The **primary barrier** in the treatment room wall must achieve a **design dose limit of 0.02 mSv/week** (for controlled area, occupational). Distance from target to barrier = **6 m**, use factor = **1/4**, occupancy factor = **1**. Lead has a TVL of **5.7 cm** at 6 MV.

### Given:
- $P = 1000\ \text{Gy·m²/week}$
- $U = 0.25$
- $P_{lim} = 0.02\ \text{mSv/week} = 0.00002\ \text{Sv/week}$
- $d = 6\ \text{m}$
- $TVL = 5.7\ \text{cm}$

### Step 1: Apply NCRP 147 Formula
$$x = TVL \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{P \cdot U}{P_{lim} \cdot d^{2}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Substitute
$$x = 5.7 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{1000 \times 0.25}{0.00002 \times 36}\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute Numerator and Denominator
- Numerator: $1000 \times 0.25 = 250$
- Denominator: $0.00002 \times 36 = 0.00072$

### Step 4: Compute the Ratio
$$\frac{250}{0.00072} = 347{,}222.22$$

### Step 5: Take Base-10 Logarithm
$$\log_{10}(347{,}222.22) = 5.541$$

### Step 6: Final Thickness
$$x = 5.7 \times 5.541 = 31.58\ \text{cm}$$

### Step 7: Add 1 HVL for Safety (NCRP Recommendation)
$$x_{final} = 31.58 + 5.7/3.32 = 31.58 + 1.72 = 33.30\ \text{cm}$$

$$\boxed{x_{final} \approx 33.3\ \text{cm of lead equivalent (or 200 cm of standard concrete at 2.35 g/cm³)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Real LINAC bunkers use **~2.0 m of ordinary concrete** or **~1.5 m of barite concrete** (3.2 g/cm³) or **~33 cm of lead** — each is mathematically equivalent. The choice depends on **cost, space, and structural loading** of the building.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Protective Devices

| Load Type | Fault Current (kA) | Recommended Breaker | Trip Setting | Coordination |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Main LT Bus (1000 kVA)** | ~25 | ACB, 40 kA Icu | L = 1.0×I_FL, S = 10×, I = 12× | Master trip |
| **Sub-distribution (250 kVA)** | ~10 | MCCB, 25 kA Icu | L = 0.9×I_FL | With ACB: 0.3 s delay |
| **Final distribution (40 A)** | ~5 | MCB, 10 kA Icu | C-curve (5–10×I_n) | With MCCB: 0.1 s delay |
| **OT panel (Isolated Power)** | < 0.05 (limited) | — | Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) | Per IEC 60364-7-710 |
| **Radiation room (HV equipment)** | < 1 | MCB + SPD Type 1+2 | C-curve, 30 mA ELCB | Per IEC 60364-4-44 |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Hospital Earth Electrode Resistance (Rod in Soil)

### Formula (Dwight's Empirical Equation for a vertical rod):
$$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

### Problem:
A hospital substation has a **3 m long, 50 mm diameter** copper-clad earth rod driven into soil with **resistivity ρ = 100 Ω·m**. Compute earth resistance.

### Step 1: Substitute Values
- $L = 3\ \text{m}$
- $d = 0.05\ \text{m}$
- $\rho = 100\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$

### Step 2: Compute Ratio
$$\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 3}{0.05} = 240$$

### Step 3: Logarithm
$$\ln(240) = 5.481$$

### Step 4: Bracket Term
$$5.481 - 1 = 4.481$$

### Step 5: Denominator
$$2\pi L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3 = 18.85$$

### Step 6: Final Resistance
$$R = \frac{100 \times 4.481}{18.85} = \frac{448.1}{18.85}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 23.77\ \Omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hospital Specification:** Single rod gives **R ≈ 24 Ω**, but hospitals require **R ≤ 1 Ω**. Therefore, we need **multiple rods in parallel** (typically 6–10 rods at 6 m spacing) plus an earth mat, achieving the mandated 1 Ω.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hospital Substation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Utility Grid<br/>11 kV / 22 kV HT Supply] --> B[HT Switchgear<br/>VCB Panel]
    B --> C[Power Transformer<br/>1000 kVA, 11kV/433V]
    C --> D[Main LT Bus<br/>433V, 3-Phase, 4-Wire]
    D --> E[ACB Master Breaker<br/>Icu = 40 kA]
    E --> F[DG Synchronization Panel]
    F --> G[Diesel Generator<br/>Standby 1000 kVA]
    F --> H[Utility Return]
    D --> I[Section 1: Critical Care Panel]
    D --> J[Section 2: OT / Cath Lab Panel]
    D --> K[Section 3: General Power Panel]
    D --> L[Section 4: Lighting Panel]
    I --> M[UPS - Online Double Conversion<br/>60 kVA, 30 min backup]
    J --> N[Isolated Power System<br/>IPS for Group 2]
    M --> O[ICU / NICU Loads]
    N --> P[OT / Cath Lab / CCU Loads]
    K --> Q[AHU, Pumps, Lifts]
    L --> R[Corridor, OPD, Admin]
```

> **Node Legend:**
> - **HT Switchgear (VCB):** Vacuum Circuit Breaker at 11 kV for safe isolation.
> - **Synchronization Panel:** Ensures seamless ATSS (Auto Transfer Switch) within < 10 s per IS 10500.
> - **IPS (Isolated Power System):** Mandatory for Group 2 medical locations — limits first-fault leakage to **< 50 µA**, preventing microshock.

---

## 4.2 UPS System Block Diagram (Online Double Conversion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mains Input<br/>230V AC] --> B[Input Filter<br/>EMI/RFI Suppression]
    B --> C[Rectifier<br/>AC to DC]
    C --> D[DC Bus<br/>192V to 480V]
    D --> E[Battery Bank<br/>VRLA / Li-ion]
    D --> F[Inverter<br/>DC to AC via IGBT]
    F --> G[Static Bypass Switch<br/>SCR-based, 4ms transfer]
    F --> H[Output Filter<br/>Sine Wave Conditioning]
    H --> I[Critical Medical Load]
    J[Maintenance Bypass] -.Manual.-> I
    G -.Automatic on Inverter Fault.-> A
```

> **Operational Notes:**
> - In **normal mode**, mains → rectifier → DC bus → inverter → load (no break).
> - On **mains failure**, battery → DC bus → inverter → load (transition time = **0 ms**).
> - On **inverter failure**, static bypass connects mains directly to load in **< 4 ms**.

---

## 4.3 Protection Coordination Scheme (Discrimination)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Utility 11 kV Feeder] --> B[HT Circuit Breaker<br/>50 kA, O/C + E/F Relay]
    B --> C[Transformer 11/0.433 kV]
    C --> D[Main LT ACB<br/>40 kA, IDMT Relay]
    D --> E[MCCB-1: Critical Panel<br/>25 kA]
    D --> F[MCCB-2: General Panel<br/>25 kA]
    E --> G[MCB-A: ICU Feeder<br/>10 kA, C-Curve]
    E --> H[MCB-B: OT Feeder<br/>10 kA, B-Curve]
    F --> I[MCB-C: AHU Feeder<br/>10 kA, D-Curve]
    F --> J[MCB-D: Lighting Feeder<br/>10 kA, C-Curve]
    G --> K[Patient Monitor]
    H --> L[Ventilator / OT Light]
    I --> M[Chiller / Pump]
    J --> N[Fluorescent / LED Light]
```

> **Discrimination Principle:** Each downstream device must trip **before** the upstream device for any fault in its zone. This is achieved through **time grading** (0.05 s, 0.1 s, 0.3 s, 0.5 s) and **current grading** (1×, 1.6×, 2.5×, 4×).

---

## 4.4 Radiation Room Zoning & Safety Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Outer Zone: Public Area<br/>Dose Limit 1 mSv/year] -->|Controlled Access| B[Middle Zone: Staff Controlled<br/>Dose 20 mSv/year avg]
    B -->|Shielded Door Interlock| C[Inner Zone: Treatment Room<br/>Dose Controlled ALARA]
    C --> D[Primary Barrier<br/>Concrete 2m / Lead 33cm]
    C --> E[Secondary Barrier<br/>Scattered Radiation]
    C --> F[Maze Entrance<br/>Door Interlock + Warning Light]
    C --> G[Patient Positioning Area<br/>6-axis Robotic Couch]
    C --> H[Console Area<br/>Outside Room, Lead Glass Window]
    H --> I[Operator Console<br/>Video Surveillance + Audio]
    C -.Emergency.-> J[Emergency Stop Button]
    C -.Monitoring.-> K[Area Monitor + Dosimeter]
```

> **Safety Layers in Radiation Room:**
> 1. **Architectural Shielding** (concrete/lead walls)
> 2. **Door Interlock System** (mechanical + electrical)
> 3. **Warning Lights & Signs** (radiation symbol, illuminated "X-RAY ON")
> 4. **Area Radiation Monitors** (continuous real-time)
> 5. **Personal Dosimeters** (TLD / OSL badges for staff)
> 6. **Emergency Off (EMO)** buttons (one inside, one outside)
> 7. **Console Lockout** — beam cannot fire unless door is closed and operator confirms.

---

## 4.5 Patient Electrical Safety Network (Isolated Power System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Isolating Transformer<br/>230V/230V, 5-10 kVA] --> B[Isolated Power System Bus<br/>Unearthed IT]
    B --> C[Insulation Monitoring Device<br/>IMD per IEC 61557-8]
    B --> D[Overload Protection<br/>MCB on Secondary]
    B --> E[Line Isolation Monitor<br/>LIM, threshold 5kΩ]
    B --> F[Medical Load 1<br/>Ventilator]
    B --> G[Medical Load 2<br/>Monitor]
    B --> H[Medical Load 3<br/>Infusion Pump]
    C -.Alarm.-> I[Alarm Panel<br/>Visual + Audible]
    E -.Alarm.-> I
    I --> J[Nursing Station]
    B -.First Fault.-> K[Current Limited to < 50 µA]
    K -.Safe.-> L[Patient on Catheter]
```

> **Why IPS?** In a normal TN-S system, the **first earth fault** trips a protective device. But in a Group 2 medical location (e.g., cardiac cath lab), a **first fault must NOT trip** — otherwise, the patient on life support could die. Instead, the **IMD/LIM** raises an alarm so the medical team can fix the fault while the equipment continues running. The current is naturally limited to **< 50 µA** (below microshock threshold).

---

## 4.6 EMI Filter Functional Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Mains<br/>with Conducted EMI] --> B[SPD Type 1<br/>Lightning Arrester]
    B --> C[Line Filter<br/>Common Mode + Differential Mode Choke]
    C --> D[Isolation Transformer<br/>Shielded Winding]
    D --> E[SPD Type 2<br/>Class II Surge]
    E --> F[Power Supply Unit<br/>SMPS / PSU]
    F --> G[SPD Type 3<br/>Near Sensitive Load]
    G --> H[Medical Equipment<br/>MRI, CT, Patient Monitor]
```

> **EMI Suppression Strategy:** Use a **layered approach** — common-mode chokes (X-capacitors + Y-capacitors), shielded transformers, and SPDs at every transition point. Critical for **MRI rooms** where RF interference can corrupt image quality and **life-support equipment** where EMI can cause false alarms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following with one-line significance in hospital context:**
(i) **Medical IT System (Isolated Power System)**
(ii) **ALARA principle**
(iii) **TVL (Tenth Value Layer)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Medical IT System (Isolated Power System):**
A power system in which the **neutral of the secondary winding of an isolating transformer is unearthed (IT system)**, equipped with an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)**. The first insulation fault does not cause disconnection but raises an alarm, ensuring continuity of supply for life-support equipment in **Group 2 medical locations** (per IEC 60364-7-710).

**(ii) ALARA Principle:**
**"As Low As Reasonably Achievable"** — the fundamental radiation protection principle (ICRP) requiring that occupational and public doses be kept as far below the regulatory limits as **economic, social, and technical** factors permit.

**(iii) TVL (Tenth Value Layer):**
The thickness of a specified material (e.g., lead, concrete) that, when introduced into the path of a radiation beam, **reduces its intensity to one-tenth (10%)** of the original value. Used to design radiation barriers per NCRP 147.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Type BF and Type CF medical equipment with respect to patient safety. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Type BF** (Body Floating) | **Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient Contact** | Skin / Body surface only (no direct heart contact) | Direct contact with the **heart** |
| **Patient Leakage (Normal)** | ≤ 100 µA | **≤ 10 µA (10× more stringent)** |
| **Patient Leakage (Single Fault)** | ≤ 500 µA | **≤ 50 µA** |
| **Isolation** | Floating (defibrillation-proof, NOT cardiac) | Floating + Cardiac-grade + Defibrillation-proof |
| **Examples** | **Ultrasound machine, TENS unit, infrared lamp, BP monitor** | **Pacemaker programmer, intracardiac catheter, intra-aortic balloon pump, ECG (diagnostic, with chest electrodes)** |
| **Required Location** | General ward, OPD, examination room | **Cath lab, OT, CCU, ICU** — must be used with **Isolated Power System (IPS)** |

**Significance:** Type CF is the **most stringent** category because even 10 µA directly through the heart can trigger **ventricular fibrillation**, which is fatal.

---

## 📚 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **Online Double-Conversion UPS system**. State its **transfer time**, **efficiency**, and **two advantages** over Offline UPS for hospital use.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **1000 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V** transformer (Z = 5.5%) feeds a hospital LT panel. A 3-phase feeder of length **80 m** uses a **4-core 240 mm² XLPE cable** (R = 0.124 Ω/km, X = 0.072 Ω/km) to feed an OT panel with a **load of 250 A at 0.85 power factor lagging**. Calculate:
(i) **Full-load current** of the transformer
(ii) **Symmetrical short circuit current** at the LT bus
(iii) **Percentage voltage drop** in the OT feeder

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Online Double-Conversion UPS:**

A UPS in which the **rectifier, DC bus, and inverter are always in line** with the load, regardless of mains availability. The load receives power **only through the inverter** — never directly from the mains.

**Block Diagram:**
- **Stage 1: Rectifier** — Converts incoming 230 V/433 V AC to regulated DC (192 V to 480 V DC).
- **Stage 2: DC Bus** — Connected to the **battery bank** (VRLA/Li-ion).
- **Stage 3: Inverter (IGBT-based)** — Converts DC back to pure sine-wave AC.
- **Stage 4: Static Bypass** — SCR-based, kicks in only on inverter failure (< 4 ms transfer).
- **Stage 5: Output Filter** — Smooths harmonics.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Transfer time:** **0 ms** (truly online, no break, since load is always on inverter)
- **Efficiency:** **90–96%** (eco-mode up to 99%, but with reduced protection)
- **THD output:** **< 3%** (pure sine wave)

**Two Advantages over Offline UPS for hospital use:**
1. **Zero transfer time** — critical for ventilators, monitors, infusion pumps; offline UPS has 5–10 ms break.
2. **Complete isolation from mains noise** — eliminates spikes, sags, harmonics, frequency variations; offline passes them through.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 1000\ \text{kVA}$, $V_{HV} = 11{,}000\ \text{V}$, $V_{LV} = 433\ \text{V}$, $Z_{\%} = 5.5$, $L = 0.08\ \text{km}$, $I_{load} = 250\ \text{A}$, $PF = 0.85$, $R = 0.124\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $X = 0.072\ \Omega/\text{km}$.

**(i) Full-Load Current:**
$$I_{FL} = \frac{S \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{LL}} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{750.05} = 1333.3\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Symmetrical Short Circuit Current:**
$$I_{sc} = \frac{I_{FL}}{Z_{\%}/100} = \frac{1333.3}{0.055} = 24{,}242.4\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{sc} \approx 24.24\ \text{kA}$$

**(iii) Percentage Voltage Drop:**
First, total impedance of the cable:
$$R_{total} = 0.124 \times 0.08 = 0.00992\ \Omega$$
$$X_{total} = 0.072 \times 0.08 = 0.00576\ \Omega$$

Compute the term $(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$:
- $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} = 0.5268$

$$R\cos\phi = 0.00992 \times 0.85 = 0.008432\ \Omega$$
$$X\sin\phi = 0.00576 \times 0.5268 = 0.003034\ \Omega$$
$$R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi = 0.011466\ \Omega$$

Apply voltage drop formula:
$$\Delta V\% = \frac{I \times L \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times 100}{V_{LL}^{2}} \times 1000$$

Wait — using the correct per-phase form, for a 3-phase line:
$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times I \times (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) \times L$$
$$\Delta V = 1.732 \times 250 \times 0.011466 = 4.96\ \text{V}$$

In percentage:
$$\Delta V\% = \frac{4.96}{433} \times 100 = 1.146\%$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V\% \approx 1.15\%}$$

This is **well within the 3% permissible limit for medical IT feeders** per IEC 60364-7-710. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an Isolated Power System (IPS)** used in **Group 2 medical locations**. Why is it preferred over TN-S for OTs? List **three leakage current limits** from IEC 60601-1 for **Type CF** equipment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** bunker has workload **1500 Gy·m²/week** at 6 MV, distance to primary barrier **= 5 m**, use factor **= 1/4**, design dose limit **= 0.02 mSv/week** (controlled area).
