KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List the physical and chemical properties of Nitrous Oxide (N₂O) that are relevant to its storage and pipeline distribution in hospitals. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Molecular weight:** 44.013 g/mol → used to calculate specific gravity (1.527, heavier than air). - **Critical temperature:** 36.41 °C → below this, N₂O can be liquefied; hence in tropical Indian conditions, cylinders always contain both liquid and gas phases. - **Cylinder service pressure:** 44 bar at 21 °C → used to size high-pressure regulators and manifold design pressure. - **Colour code:** Blue body with white shoulder (IS/ISO standard) → for cylinder identification. - **Non-flammable but oxidising** → influences fire-safety zoning in MGPS rooms. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is a "hypoxic guard" mandatory in any N₂O delivery system? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A hypoxic guard ensures that the **fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO₂) never falls below 0.25 (25%)** when N₂O is being delivered. Because N₂O displaces oxygen in the breathing circuit, an incorrect setting (e.g., N₂O = 80%, O₂ = 20%) would deliver a **hypoxic mixture** (low pO₂) leading to brain damage or death. The hypoxic guard uses **mechanical interlock and pneumatic logic** to physically prevent the N₂O flowmeter from being set higher than 75% (i.e., 3:1 N₂O:O₂ ratio). This is mandated by **IS 15325** and **HTM 02-01** for all anaesthetic workstations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Module 3 Choice]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an Automatic Manifold system for N₂O cylinders in a hospital. How is uninterrupted supply ensured?** **Model Solution:** **1. Components of the Automatic Manifold (5 key elements):** - **Cylinders** (in "n+1" configuration per bank). - **High-pressure tailpipes** with NRVs to prevent backflow. - **High-pressure header** (manifold bar) for each bank. - **Pressure sensors / transducers** on each bank header. - **Automatic changeover regulator** with solenoid valves or differential-pressure mechanism. **2. Working Logic — Step by Step:** 1. Initially, **Bank A (left)** is "in service" and **Bank B (right)** is "reserve". 2. The regulator downstream of Bank A maintains line pressure at **4.1 bar**. 3. As cylinders in Bank A deplete, the **header pressure drops**. 4. When header pressure falls below a preset threshold (typically **7 bar**), the **automatic changeover valve** switches flow to **Bank B**. 5. Simultaneously, a **low-pressure alarm** is sent to the central MGPS panel and the local manifold enclosure, signalling that Bank A cylinders need replacement. 6. Bank A is replenished (full cylinders swapped in) and becomes the new "reserve" bank. 7. The cycle repeats indefinitely, providing **uninterrupted 24×7 supply**. **3. Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above):** Bank A → Pressure Switches → Changeover Valve → Bank B → Pressure Switches → Master Regulator → MGPS Line. **Key Standards Followed:** IS 15325:2003, HTM 02-01, NFPA 99. **Redundancy Formula (n+1):** $N = n + 1$ — one extra cylinder beyond the calculated need is always held in the in-use bank as an immediate spare. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A hospital has 6 operating tables. Each table consumes N₂O at 5 L/min during surgery. Surgery runs 8 hours/day, 6 days/week. The manifold uses Size-G cylinders (capacity 4960 L gas). Calculate the number of cylinders per bank and the total cylinders in the manifold. Assume weekly changeover with n+1 redundancy.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Daily demand per table:** $$ D_{table} = 5 \text{ L/min} \times 8 \text{ h/day} \times 60 \text{ min/h} = 2400 \text{ L/day} $$ **Step 2 — Weekly demand (6 tables, 6 days):** $$ D_{weekly} = 6 \times 2400 \times 6 = 86{,}400 \text{ L/week} $$ **Step 3 — Cylinders per bank (n):** $$ n = \left\lceil \frac{86400}{4960} \right\rceil = \lceil 17.42 \rceil = 18 \text{ cylinders} $$ **Step 4 — n+1 redundancy:** $$ N_{bank} = 18 + 1 = 19 \text{ cylinders per bank} $$ **Step 5 — Total manifold capacity (bank A + bank B):** $$ N_{total} = 2 \times 19 = 38 \text{ cylinders} $$ **Result:** The hospital requires a **38-cylinder automatic manifold (19 in-use + 19 reserve)** to ensure uninterrupted N₂O supply for the 6-OT complex. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mark-Loss Warning:** > - ❌ **Forgetting the +1 spare cylinder** → examiners deduct 2 marks. > - ❌ **Confusing "L/day" vs "L/week"** → leads to wrong cylinder count. > - ❌ **Not converting L/min to L/week** correctly (forgetting × 60 × hours × days). > - ✅ Always state the **n+1 assumption explicitly** to earn the methodology mark. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Module 3 Choice]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Describe the manufacturing process of medical-grade N₂O. What impurities must be removed, and what is the role of the Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP) standards?** **Model Solution:** **1. Industrial Manufacturing Method — Thermal Decomposition of Ammonium Nitrate:** The dominant route is the **thermal cracking (decomposition) of ammonium nitrate**: $$ NH_4NO_3 \xrightarrow{200-260^{\circ}C} N_2O + 2H_2O $$ The reaction is endothermic, occurs in a stainless-steel reactor, and the gaseous N₂O is passed through a series of **purification columns**. **2. Purification Steps (in sequence):** - **Scrubber (water wash):** Removes residual NH₃ and HNO₃. - **Alkaline scrubber (NaOH):** Removes CO₂ and acidic impurities. - **Drying tower (silica gel / molecular sieve):** Removes moisture to dew point **< −60 °C**. - **Activated carbon filter:** Removes higher oxides of nitrogen (NO₂, NO). - **Cold trap (LN₂-cooled):** Removes trace impurities like N₂O₄. **3. Quality Standards (IP / USP / BP):** - **N₂O purity:** ≥ 99.0% (IP), ≥ 99.5% (USP). - **Maximum allowable limits:** CO < 10 ppm, NO/NO₂ < 1 ppm, halogens (Cl⁻) < 5 ppm, water < 50 ppm. - **Identity test:** Confirms N₂O via combustion support test (glowing splint re-ignites). **4. Cylinder Filling:** The purified gas is **compressed to ~44 bar** and **liquefied** into cleaned, hydrostatically tested cylinders. Each cylinder is leak-tested, vacuum-baked, and labelled with batch number, expiry (typically 36 months), and the **IS colour code**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A 50 m DN 22 mm copper pipeline carries N₂O at 60 L/min. The Hazen-Williams coefficient is 130. Calculate the pressure drop in kPa. If the working pressure is 4.1 bar, is this design acceptable per HTM 02-01 (max 5% drop)?** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $Q = 60$ L/min = $60 \times 10^{-3} / 60$ m³/s = $1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s - $d = 0.022$ m - $L = 50$ m - $C = 130$ **Step 1 — Apply Hazen-Williams:** $$ \Delta P = \frac{4.52 \times Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \times d^{4.87}} \times L $$ **Step 2 — Numerator:** $$ Q^{1.85} = (1 \times 10^{-3})^{1.85} = 1 \times 10^{-5.55} = 2.818 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \text{Num} = 4.52 \times 2.818 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 6.37 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3 — Denominator:** $$ C^{1.85} = 130^{1.85} \approx 9954 $$ $$ d^{4.87} = (0.022)^{4.87} \approx 1.09 \times 10^{-13} $$ $$ \text{Den} = 9954 \times 1.09 \times 10^{-13} = 1.085 \times 10^{-9} $$ **Step 4 — Compute ΔP:** $$ \Delta P = \frac{6.37 \times 10^{-4}}{1.085 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.87 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 587 \text{ kPa} = 5.87 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 5 — Compare with HTM 02-01 limit:** - 5% of 4.1 bar = 0.205 bar (205 kPa) - Actual drop = 5.87 bar - **Excess drop = 5.87 / 0.205 = 28.6 × the limit** **Conclusion:** The design is **NOT acceptable**. The pipe diameter must be increased to at least **DN 35 mm or DN 42 mm** to bring the pressure drop below 0.2 bar. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mark-Loss Warning:** > - ❌ **Mixing units** (using L/min directly without converting to m³/s) → loses 1 mark. > - ❌ **Not applying the 5% rule** → loses the "design acceptability" mark. > - ❌ **Forgetting to state the design recommendation** (e.g., "increase to DN 35") → loses 1 conclusion mark. > - ✅ Always quote the **HTM 02-01 / IS 15325 reference** for the 5% rule. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Nitrous Oxide):** - **Formula of N₂O:** Di-nitrogen monoxide, MW = 44.013 g/mol. - **Colour code (cylinder):** Blue body, white shoulder. - **Cylinder service pressure:** 44 bar at 21 °C. - **Line working pressure (MGPS):** 4.1 bar (60 psig). - **Critical temperature:** 36.41 °C — N₂O is stored as liquefied gas in cylinders at all hospital ambient temperatures in Kerala. - **Manufacturing method:** Thermal decomposition of ammonium nitrate at 200–260 °C → $NH_4NO_3 \rightarrow N_2O + 2H_2O$. - **Purification stages:** Scrubber → Alkaline wash → Drying → Activated carbon → Cold trap. - **Purity standard:** ≥ 99.0% as per Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP). - **Medical uses:** Inhalational anaesthetic (MAC ≈ 104 vol%), analgesic, procedural sedation. - **Hypoxic guard rule:** FiO₂ ≥ 0.25 always; mechanical interlock prevents N₂O > 75%. - **Manifold design:** n+1 redundancy (in-use + reserve) — mirrored left/right bank with 7 bar changeover. - **Cylinder duration formula:** $t = V_{c} / Q$ (or use $t = M_L / (Q \cdot \rho_v)$ for liquid basis). - **Manifold sizing formula:** $N_{cyl} = \lceil (D_{weekly} / V_c) \rceil + 1$. - **Pipeline material:** Seamless DHP-grade copper, joined by silver-brazing (BS EN 13348). - **Pipe colour:** Blue with white shoulder markings (per IS 2379 / IS 15325). - **Pipeline pressure drop limit:** ≤ 5% of working pressure (HTM 02-01 / NFPA 99). - **Hazen-Williams equation (SI):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$. - **Specific gravity of N₂O:** 1.527 (heavier than air) → pooling hazard in unventilated low-lying areas. - **Scavenging system:** Active Anaesthetic Gas Scavenging System (AGSS) is mandatory in OTs to prevent occupational exposure (N₂O exposure limit = 25 ppm TWA per NIOSH). - **Famous KTU trick:** "Why N₂O is non-flammable but supports combustion" → it is a strong oxidiser, so it accelerates combustion of other fuels without itself burning. - **Mnemonic for Cylinder colour code:** "**N**itrous is **N**eptune → **B**lue" (Neptune, the blue planet). > [!WARNING] > **Last-Minute Pitfalls Before KTU Exam:** > 1. **Don't confuse** N₂O with NO (nitric oxide) — NO is a colourless toxic gas; N₂O is a medical anaesthetic. > 2. **Don't mix** the cylinder service pressure (44 bar) with the MGPS line pressure (4.1 bar). > 3. **Don't forget** to convert L/min → m³/s in any Hazen-Williams calculation. > 4. **Always state the n+1 assumption** in any manifold design question. > 5. **Always reference HTM 02-01 or IS 15325** in design-validity answers to earn full marks.

topics.title: Vacuum and Oxygen - Principle of Production of Liquid Oxygen, Air Quality in Hospital Environment - Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT501 - HOSPITAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vacuum and Oxygen - Principle of Production of Liquid Oxygen, Air Quality in Hospital Environment - Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Medical Vacuum in Hospitals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** A **medical vacuum system** is a centralized, pipeline-distributed suction network that provides controlled sub-atmospheric pressure (typically **−300 mmHg to −700 mmHg**) for clinical procedures such as surgical suction, wound drainage, endotracheal aspiration, and laboratory aspiration.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital Vacuum Cleaner":**
Think of a medical vacuum pipeline as a giant, continuous "suction hose" running through every ward, OT, and ICU. Just like the small handheld vacuum a dentist uses, the central system creates a gentle, controlled suction — but instead of one motor at one chair, there is a powerful vacuum pump in the basement pushing suction through copper pipes to every bedside wall outlet. Whenever a nurse inserts the suction tip, it draws out fluids safely without any electric device near the patient.

### Key Components of a Hospital Vacuum System
| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| Vacuum pump (rotary vane / liquid ring) | Generates suction pressure |
| Vacuum receiver tank | Buffers pulsations, stores condensate |
| Bacterial filter (HEPA) | Prevents contamination of pipeline |
| Pipeline network (copper / SS) | Distributes vacuum to ward outlets |
| Vacuum regulator | Adjusts suction level at bedside |
| Trap bottles | Prevents fluid ingress into pipeline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pressure Reference:** 1 atm = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mmHg** = **1.01325 bar**. Hospital vacuum is therefore measured in negative mmHg (e.g., **−500 mmHg**).

---

## 1.2 Medical Oxygen Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Medical Oxygen (USP Grade)** is oxygen supplied at a minimum purity of **99.0% (v/v)** with critically low levels of CO, CO₂, and hydrocarbons, used for patient therapy, anesthesia, and life support. In hospitals, it is most economically stored and transported as **Liquid Medical Oxygen (LMO)** at cryogenic temperatures.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Big Thermos Flask of Life":**
Imagine boiling water to make tea. If you want to store the steam, you cool it back to liquid water. Liquid oxygen is produced the same way — we cool atmospheric air down to **−183 °C** so that oxygen condenses into a pale blue liquid. This liquid is then stored in huge insulated tanks (like giant thermos flasks called **cryogenic dewars**) and piped directly to the ICU. One litre of LMO produces **about 860 litres of gaseous oxygen** — making it incredibly space-efficient.

### Why LMO Instead of Cylinders?
| Parameter | Oxygen Cylinder (D-type) | Liquid Oxygen (1 L LMO) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage form | Gas at 200 bar | Cryogenic liquid at −183 °C |
| Equivalent O₂ | ~7,000 L | ~860 L |
| Footprint | High | Very low |
| Refill logistics | Heavy cylinder handling | Bulk tanker top-up |

---

## 1.3 Production of Liquid Oxygen — The Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Gases liquefy when cooled below their **critical temperature** under sufficient pressure. Since air's critical temperature is far below ambient, we cool air progressively using **throttling (Joule–Thomson effect)** and/or **isentropic expansion in an expander**, accumulating cooling until the components condense.

### Critical Data for Air / Oxygen (Bold for Memorization)
| Property | Oxygen (O₂) | Nitrogen (N₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **−182.97 °C** | **−195.79 °C** |
| Critical temperature | **−118.57 °C** | **−146.96 °C** |
| Critical pressure | **50.43 bar** | **33.96 bar** |

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Snowball Effect of Cooling":**
Imagine repeatedly blowing on your hand with your mouth. Each breath is slightly cooler than your body. Now imagine cycling compressed air through a heat exchanger with itself: the cold returning gas cools the fresh incoming gas. Each cycle leaves the system a little colder. After hundreds of such cycles, the temperature falls so low that oxygen (the higher-boiling component) starts raining out as liquid — exactly how an air conditioner or a refrigerator works, but engineered for −183 °C.

---

## 1.4 Air Quality in Hospital Environment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Hospital Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** refers to the controlled condition of air within clinical areas with respect to **temperature (°C), relative humidity (%RH), particulate count (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀), microbial count (CFU/m³), air changes per hour (ACH), and pressure differential (Pa)** relative to adjacent areas.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Surgical Theatre as a Sterile Bubble":**
Picture the OT as a cleanroom. Every minute, the entire volume of air is filtered and replaced **20–25 times** to flush out any germs released during surgery. The room is kept at slightly **higher pressure** than the corridor so that when the door opens, clean air rushes OUT (not contaminated corridor air IN). The opposite happens in an isolation ward for TB patients — air flows IN, never OUT, trapping pathogens inside.

---

## 1.5 Heating in Hospital Environment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Hospital heating** refers to the engineered systems that provide thermal energy for **(a) patient comfort heating**, **(b) hot water for clinical use and sterilization**, **(c) steam for autoclaves and laundry**, and **(d) process heating for kitchens and boilers**, typically delivered through **hot water boilers, steam boilers, calorifiers, and heat distribution networks**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Heating as the Hospital's Circulatory System":**
Just as blood carries warmth throughout the body, hot water and steam circulate through insulated pipes delivering heat to wards, OTs, kitchens, and sterilizers. The boiler is the "heart," pumps are the "arteries," radiators/finned-tube heaters are the "capillaries" delivering warmth, and the steam lines supply energy for autoclaves that sterilize surgical instruments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Joule–Thomson (Throttling) Effect — Foundation of Liquefaction

> [!NOTE]
> When a real gas expands through a porous plug, throttle, or partially-open valve from a high pressure to a low pressure at constant enthalpy, its temperature changes. This is the **Joule–Thomson Effect**, the basis of cryogenic air separation.

### Mathematical Definition

$$\mu_{JT} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$

where:
- $\mu_{JT}$ = Joule–Thomson coefficient (**K/bar**)
- $T$ = temperature (K)
- $P$ = pressure (bar)
- subscript $H$ = at constant enthalpy

**Significance of μJT:**
| Value of μJT | Physical Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $\mu_{JT} > 0$ | Gas cools on expansion (e.g., air at 300 K, μJT ≈ **+0.25 K/bar**) |
| $\mu_{JT} < 0$ | Gas warms on expansion (e.g., H₂, He at 300 K) |
| $\mu_{JT} = 0$ | Ideal gas — no temperature change |

**Inversion Temperature ($T_{inv}$):** Above this temperature, even a real gas warms upon expansion. For air, $T_{inv} ≈ 621$ K, so air always cools on throttling at room temperature — making liquefaction feasible.

---

## 2.2 The Simple Linde (Joule–Thomson) Cycle

### Operating Sequence (Step-by-Step)

1. **Compression (1→2):** Air is compressed isentropically from $P_1$ (≈1 bar) to $P_2$ (≈200 bar).
2. **After-cooling (2→2'):** Compressed air is cooled in the after-cooler back to ambient temperature $T_1$ at constant $P_2$.
3. **Recuperative cooling (2'→2''):** The high-pressure air passes through the **recuperator** on the hot side, transferring heat to the cold returning low-pressure stream.
4. **Throttling (2''→3):** Air is expanded isenthalpically through a throttle valve to $P_3 = P_1$.
5. **Separation (3→4 liquid + 5 vapor):** The two-phase mixture enters a flash drum; liquid oxygen is withdrawn as product; cold vapor returns through the recuperator (cold side, 5→5') back to the compressor inlet.

### Governing Equations (Energy Balances)

**Energy balance on throttle + separator** (adiabatic, no work):

$$h_{2''} \;=\; y \cdot h_f \;+\; (1 - y) \cdot h_g$$

where:
- $h_{2''}$ = enthalpy of high-pressure stream entering throttle (kJ/kg)
- $y$ = **mass fraction liquefied (yield)**
- $h_f$ = enthalpy of saturated liquid at flash pressure $P_3$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_g$ = enthalpy of saturated vapor at flash pressure $P_3$ (kJ/kg)

**Solving for yield:**

$$\boxed{\;y \;=\; \dfrac{h_{2''} - h_g}{h_f - h_g}\;}$$

**Energy balance on recuperator** (assuming perfect heat exchange and unit mass of gas compressed):

$$h_{2'} - h_{2''} \;=\; (1 - y)\,(h_{g} - h_{1})$$

This couples the inlet temperature $T_1$, the cooled high-pressure temperature $T_{2''}$, and the liquefaction yield $y$.

**Work of compression (isentropic):**

$$W_c \;=\; C_p \, T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$

**Specific work of liquefaction (work per kg of liquid produced):**

$$W_L \;=\; \dfrac{W_c}{y}$$

---

## 2.3 The Claude Cycle (Improved Liquefaction)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Claude Cycle** improves yield by replacing part of the throttling with **isentropic expansion in an expansion engine (expander)**, which extracts useful work AND produces more cooling per unit mass than throttling alone.

### Key Difference vs Simple Linde
| Feature | Simple Linde | Claude |
|---|---|---|
| Expansion device | Throttle valve only | Throttle valve + expander |
| Cooling produced | Small (only μJT effect) | Large (μJT + isentropic) |
| Yield | Low | Higher (typically 2–3×) |
| Complexity | Simple | More machinery |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. Every formula below has been asked in previous KTU University Examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu_{JT} = (\partial T / \partial P)_{H}$ | Joule–Thomson coefficient | K/bar |
| 2 | $y = (h_{2''} - h_{g}) / (h_{f} - h_{g})$ | Liquefaction yield (Linde) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_{c} = C_p T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ | Isentropic compression work | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $W_{L} = W_c / y$ | Specific work of liquefaction | kJ/kg liquid |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{heat} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load | kW |
| 6 | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer through walls | W or kW |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{ACH} = V_{room} \times N_{ACH}$ | Required ventilation flow | m³/h |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{Q}_{lat} / h_{fg}$ | Steam mass flow for process | kg/s |
| 9 | $P_{diff} = \Delta P \, (\text{positive or negative})$ | Room pressure differential | Pa |
| 10 | $\eta_{isentropic} = (h_1 - h_{2s})/(h_1 - h_{2})$ | Turbine / expander efficiency | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Hospital Oxygen Plant Design:** Engineers use the Linde yield equation to size compressors, heat exchangers, and flash columns for the desired LMO capacity (e.g., 50 kL/day).
- **Cryogenic Storage Engineering:** μJT calculations determine pre-cooling strategies for LMO tanks before first fill.
- **Energy Audits:** Heating load equations help hospitals achieve **3–4 star NABH / GRIHA ratings** by minimizing boiler fuel consumption.
- **Infection Control Compliance:** The ACH equation is mandated by **ASHRAE 170** for OTs, isolation rooms, and ICUs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liquefaction Yield for the Simple Linde Process

### Schematic Description
The system consists of a compressor, an after-cooler, a recuperative heat exchanger, a throttling valve (Joule–Thomson valve), and a flash separator (liquid–vapor receiver). The cold vapor leaving the separator flows back through the cold side of the recuperator and mixes with fresh air entering the compressor (Stream 1).

### State Point Notation
| State | Condition | Pressure | Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor inlet (mixed stream) | $P_1$ | $T_1$ (ambient ≈ 300 K) |
| 2 | Compressor outlet | $P_2$ | $T_2$ (high) |
| 2′ | After after-cooler | $P_2$ | $T_{2'} \approx T_1$ |
| 2″ | Recuperator exit (cold, high P) | $P_2$ | $T_{2''}$ (cold) |
| 3 | After throttle (two-phase) | $P_1$ | $T_3 = T_{sat}(P_1)$ |
| 4 | Saturated liquid (LOX) | $P_1$ | $T_3$ |
| 5 | Saturated vapor (return gas) | $P_1$ | $T_3$ |

### Step 1 — Steady-Flow Energy Equation on Throttle + Separator

Assuming the throttle and separator are **adiabatic** with **no work** and **negligible kinetic / potential energy changes**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} \, h_{2''} &= \dot{m}_{liq} \, h_f + \dot{m}_{vap} \, h_g \\
h_{2''} &= y \, h_f + (1 - y) \, h_g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The total enthalpy entering the throttle (per unit mass of compressed gas) equals the sum of the enthalpies carried away as liquid and vapor. We define $y = \dot{m}_{liq}/\dot{m}$ as the liquefaction fraction.

### Step 2 — Solving the Energy Equation for Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{2''} &= y \, h_f + h_g - y \, h_g \\
h_{2''} - h_g &= y \, (h_f - h_g) \\
y &= \dfrac{h_{2''} - h_g}{h_f - h_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $h_f < h_g$ and $h_{2''} > h_g$ (because the gas entering the throttle is warmer than the saturation temperature), $y$ is positive and dimensionless.

### Step 3 — Idealization for Air (Constant $C_p$ Approximation)

For air behaving as an **ideal gas** in the recuperator, enthalpy depends only on temperature:

$$
h_{2'} - h_{2''} = C_p \, (T_{2'} - T_{2''})
$$

Since $T_{2'} = T_1$ (after-cooler returns gas to ambient):

$$
h_{2'} - h_{2''} = C_p \, (T_1 - T_{2''})
$$

For the cold vapor leaving the separator at $T_3$ and warming up to $T_1$:

$$
h_g - h_1 = C_p \, (T_3 - T_1)
$$

### Step 4 — Energy Balance on the Recuperator (cold and hot sides)

Mass entering compressed side = 1 kg. Of this, $y$ kg liquefies and $(1-y)$ kg returns as cold vapor through the cold side.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{C_p (T_1 - T_{2''})}_{\text{hot side cooling}} &= (1 - y) \, \underbrace{C_p (T_3 - T_1)}_{\text{cold side heating}} \\
T_1 - T_{2''} &= (1 - y)(T_3 - T_1) \\
T_{2''} &= T_1 - (1 - y)(T_3 - T_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Yield Equation

Using ideal-gas enthalpies ($h = C_p T$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \dfrac{C_p T_{2''} - C_p T_3}{C_p T_f - C_p T_g} \\
  &= \dfrac{T_{2''} - T_3}{T_f - T_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

But $T_f = T_g = T_3$ (both liquid and vapor in the flash drum are at saturation temperature $T_3$). So the denominator becomes the **latent heat expressed as equivalent temperature difference**. A more rigorous form uses enthalpies directly.

### Step 6 — Useful Numerical Form

For air, assuming the cold vapor leaves the recuperator exactly at $T_1$ (perfect heat exchange) and the saturated vapor enthalpy is $h_g$, the **simplified yield expression** commonly used in textbooks is:

$$
\boxed{\;y \;=\; \dfrac{C_p \, (T_1 - T_{2''})}{h_g - h_f} \;=\; \dfrac{C_p \, (T_1 - T_{2''})}{h_{fg}}\;}
$$

where $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of oxygen at the flash pressure.

**Interpretation:**
- Higher $T_1 - T_{2''}$ (i.e., deeper cooling in the recuperator) → larger $y$.
- Smaller $h_{fg}$ (lower boiling point gas) → larger $y$ but harder to liquefy.
- Multiple throttling stages (cascade) drive $T_{2''}$ lower and increase overall $y$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Compression Work (Isentropic)

For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic compression from $P_1$ to $P_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1) \\
\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} &= \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \\
T_2 &= T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\;W_c \;=\; C_p \, T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]\;}
$$

For air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

### Sample Numerical Check (Conceptual — not part of any exam question)

Take $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 200$ bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 300 \times (200)^{0.4/1.4} \\
    &= 300 \times (200)^{0.2857} \\
    &\approx 300 \times 4.18 \\
    &\approx 1254 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
W_c = 1.005 \times (1254 - 300) \approx 959 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

This shows why multi-stage compression with inter-cooling is mandatory in real oxygen plants.

---

## 3.3 Heating Load Calculation for a Hospital Ward

### Given (Hypothetical)
- Ward volume: $V = 600$ m³
- Air mass flow for ventilation: $\dot{m} = 3.0$ kg/s
- Outside temperature: $T_o = 12$ °C
- Desired inside temperature: $T_i = 22$ °C
- $C_p^{air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K

### Ventilation (Infiltration) Heating Load

$$
\dot{Q}_{vent} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T = 3.0 \times 1.005 \times (22 - 12)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{vent} = 3.0 \times 1.005 \times 10 = 30.15 \; \text{kW}
$$

### Transmission Load Through Walls

$$
\dot{Q}_{wall} = U \, A \, \Delta T
$$

With $U = 0.6$ W/m²·K, $A = 250$ m², $\Delta T = 10$ K:

$$
\dot{Q}_{wall} = 0.6 \times 250 \times 10 = 1500 \; \text{W} = 1.5 \; \text{kW}
$$

### Total Heating Load

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{vent} + \dot{Q}_{wall} + \dot{Q}_{roof} + \dot{Q}_{floor}
$$

In KTU numericals, the student is expected to **sum all components** including walls, roof, floor, glass, ventilation (infiltration), and any process heat (e.g., sterilization steam) to size the boiler.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Design Parameters on Plant Performance

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on $y$ (yield) | Effect on $W_L$ (specific work) |
|---|---|---|
| Compression pressure $P_2$ | $y$ increases (more μJT cooling) | $W_L$ decreases per unit liquid |
| Recuperator effectiveness | $y$ increases (colder $T_{2''}$) | $W_L$ decreases |
| Ambient $T_1$ | $y$ decreases (less cooling available) | $W_L$ increases |
| Number of throttling stages | $y$ increases | $W_L$ decreases |
| Use of expander (Claude) | $y$ increases significantly | $W_L$ decreases substantially |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Simple Linde Process — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Air Inlet Ambient T1 P1] --> A2[Compressor 1 to 2]
    A2 --> A3[After Cooler 2 to 2 prime]
    A3 --> A4[Recuperator Hot Side 2 prime to 2 double prime]
    A4 --> A5[Throttle Valve 2 double prime to 3]
    A5 --> A6[Flash Separator 3 to 4 and 5]
    A6 --> A7[Liquid Oxygen Product 4]
    A6 --> A8[Cold Vapor Return 5]
    A8 --> A9[Recuperator Cold Side 5 to 5 prime]
    A9 --> A1
    style A7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style A5 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **A1 → A2 → A3**: Fresh air is compressed and cooled back to ambient.
- **A3 → A4**: The high-pressure gas enters the **recuperator hot side**.
- **A4 → A5**: Cold compressed gas passes through the **throttle (JT valve)** — this is where cooling is generated.
- **A5 → A6**: Two-phase mixture enters the **flash separator**.
- **A7**: **Liquid oxygen (LOX)** withdrawn as product.
- **A8 → A9 → A1**: Cold vapor **recuperates** its cold energy to incoming high-pressure gas, then returns to compressor inlet.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Joule–Thomson coefficient. State its significance in the production of liquid oxygen.**

**Model Answer:**
The Joule–Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with pressure at constant enthalpy:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$

For an ideal gas, $\mu_{JT} = 0$. For real gases, $\mu_{JT}$ depends on temperature and pressure. For air at room temperature, $\mu_{JT} \approx +0.25$ K/bar, which means air **cools upon expansion** through a throttle. This cooling effect is **accumulated** in the simple Linde process — air is compressed, cooled to ambient, then throttled, and the cold vapor is used to pre-cool the next batch. Repeating this cycle causes progressive cooling until the air temperature falls below the critical temperature of oxygen (−118.57 °C), at which point liquid oxygen begins to condense. Hence the Joule–Thomson effect is the **fundamental physical principle** that enables liquefaction of oxygen in cryogenic plants.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the important air quality parameters maintained in a hospital operating theatre as per ASHRAE 170 / NABH standards.**

**Model Answer:**
The following air quality parameters are mandated for a hospital operating theatre:

| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **21–24 °C** |
| Relative humidity | **30–60 % RH** (often 40–60 %) |
| Air changes per hour (ACH) | **20–25 ACH** |
| Filtration | **HEPA H13 (≥ 99.97 % at 0.3 μm)** |
| Positive pressure differential | **+12.5 Pa** relative to corridor |
| Airflow pattern | **Unidirectional (laminar)**, 0.3–0.5 m/s |
| Microbial load (CFU/m³) | **< 35 CFU/m³** during operation |

These parameters collectively minimize surgical site infections and maintain a sterile environment.

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the yield of liquid oxygen in the simple Linde process. State the assumptions.** [7 marks]

**(b) With a neat flow diagram, explain the Claude cycle for the liquefaction of air and compare it with the simple Linde process.** [7 marks]

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Aim:** Derive $y = (h_{2''} - h_g)/(h_f - h_g)$ for the simple Linde process.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation; all kinetic and potential energy changes negligible.
2. Throttle and separator are perfectly insulated (adiabatic).
3. Air behaves as an ideal gas with constant $C_p$ in the recuperator.
4. The cold vapor leaves the recuperator at the compressor inlet temperature $T_1$ (perfect heat exchange).
5. The flash drum separates saturated liquid and saturated vapor at pressure $P_1$.

**Derivation:**

*Step 1 — Energy balance on throttle + flash separator (no work, adiabatic):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} \, h_{2''} &= \dot{m}_{liq} \, h_f + \dot{m}_{vap} \, h_g \\
h_{2''} &= y \, h_f + (1 - y) \, h_g
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $y$ :*

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{2''} - h_g &= y \, (h_f - h_g) \\
y &= \dfrac{h_{2''} - h_g}{h_f - h_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Energy balance on the recuperator (hot side = cold side, ignoring $y$ since it is small):*

$$
C_p (T_1 - T_{2''}) = (1 - y) \, C_p (T_g - T_1)
$$

$$
T_{2''} = T_1 - (1 - y)(T_g - T_1)
$$

*Step 4 — Substitute $h = C_p T$ into the yield equation:*

$$
y = \dfrac{C_p (T_{2''} - T_g)}{C_p (T_f - T_g)} = \dfrac{T_{2''} - T_g}{T_f - T_g}
$$

Since $T_f = T_g$ at saturation temperature $T_3$, we use **enthalpies** directly to retain non-zero denominator:

$$
\boxed{\;y = \dfrac{h_{2''} - h_g}{h_f - h_g} = \dfrac{C_p (T_{2''} - T_g)}{h_f - h_g}\;}
$$

*Step 5 — Interpretation:*
- Higher compression ratio $P_2/P_1$ → larger $(T_1 - T_{2''})$ → larger $y$.
- Perfect recuperator effectiveness → maximum $y$.

**(7 marks — 1 mark assumptions + 4 marks derivation + 2 marks interpretation/diagram.)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Claude Cycle — Description:**

The **Claude Cycle** is an improved cryogenic liquefaction cycle in which part of the high-pressure air is expanded through a **reciprocating or turbo expander** instead of a throttle valve. The isentropic expansion in the expander extracts **work** and produces **more cooling per kg** than throttling alone, significantly improving liquefaction efficiency.

**Flow Sequence:**

1. Air is compressed (Stage A).
2. After-cooling returns compressed air to ambient (Stage B).
3. A portion ($\sim 70\%$) of the air is sent through the recuperator (Stage C).
4. The remaining $\sim 30\%$ is sent to an **expander** producing cold gas and work (Stage D).
5. Cold streams from both branches mix in the flash separator.
6. Liquid oxygen is withdrawn; cold vapor returns to recuperator.

**Claude Cycle Flow Diagram (Reference Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Air Inlet] --> D2[Compressor]
    D2 --> D3[After Cooler]
    D3 --> D4[Splitter]
    D4 --> D5[Recuperator]
    D4 --> D6[Expander Engine]
    D5 --> D7[Throttle Valve]
    D6 --> D8[Flash Separator]
    D7 --> D8
    D8 --> D9[Liquid Oxygen]
    D8 --> D10[Cold Vapor Return]
    D10 --> D5
    style D6 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Simple Linde | Claude |
|---|---|---|
| Expansion device | Throttle valve only | Expander + throttle |
| Cooling produced per kg | Small (μJT only) | Large (μJT + isentropic) |
| Work recovered | None | Yes (drives auxiliaries) |
| Yield | Lower | Higher (≈ 2–3 ×) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Maintenance | Simple | More complex |

**(7 marks — 2 marks diagram + 3 marks working + 2 marks comparison.)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Air Quality Parameters & Pressure Differentials in Hospital Areas:**

The HVAC system of a hospital is designed based on the **clinical function** of each area. ASHRAE Standard 170 / ISHRAE guidelines specify the following:

| Area | Temp (°C) | RH (%) | ACH | Pressure (Pa) | Filtration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Theatre | 21–24 | 30–60 | 20–25 | +12.5 (positive) | HEPA H13 |
| ICU | 21–24 | 30–60 | ≥ 12 | +10 (positive) | HEPA H13 |
| Isolation (Airborne) | 21–24 | 30–60 | ≥ 12 | −12.5 (negative) | HEPA H13 |
| General Ward | 22–26 | 30–60 | 4–6 | Neutral | F8 |
| Pharmacy | 21–24 | 35–50 | 4 | Positive | F8 |
| Kitchen | 26–28 | < 70 | 15–20 | Negative | Mesh filter |

**Pressure Relationship Principle:**
- **Operating Theatre:** Positive pressure prevents contaminated corridor air from entering when doors open.
- **Isolation Room (TB, COVID):** Negative pressure prevents pathogens from leaking out.
- **ICU:** Slightly positive to protect immunocompromised patients.
- **Anteroom:** Acts as buffer between positive and negative zones.

**Other critical parameters:**
- **Airflow pattern:** Unidirectional (laminar) in OT to sweep particles away from the surgical site.
- **Microbial load:** < 35 CFU/m³ in OT during operation.
- **Recovery time:** Time to clear 99 % of particles — typically ≤ 15–20 min.

**(7 marks — 3 marks table + 2 marks pressure logic + 2 marks special parameters.)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Heating Systems in Hospitals:**

1. **Hot Water Heating System (LTHW — Low Temperature Hot Water, 80–95 °C):**
   - Hot water boilers heat water and circulate it via pumps.
   - Used for **space heating** through **finned tube heaters, radiators, and radiant panels**.
   - Preferred for wards, ICUs, OTs (no combustion in occupied zones).

2. **Steam Heating System (3–7 bar saturated steam):**
   - Used where high temperatures are needed — **autoclaves (CSSD)**, **laundry**, **kitchen cooking**, **sterilizers**, **steam humidifiers**.
   - Steam gives up latent heat $h_{fg}$ on condensation (~2100 kJ/kg).

3. **Calorifier / Indirect DHW:**
   - A heat exchanger that heats domestic hot water using boiler hot water — prevents cross-contamination.

4. **Electric Heating:** Used in small zones, OT laminar flow units, infant warmers, blood warmers.

5. **Heat Pumps (Air-Source / Water-Source):** Used in modern green hospitals for energy efficiency.

**Applications Table:**

| System | Hospital Application |
|---|---|
| LTHW + Finned heaters | Wards, OPD waiting |
| Steam (autoclave) | CSSD instrument sterilization |
| Steam (laundry) | Hospital laundry |
| Steam humidifier | OT and ICU humidity control |
| Calorifier | Patient shower water |
