### Heating Load Estimation for the Given Ward

**Given data summary:**
- Volume $V = 600$ m³
- $A_{wall} = 200$ m², $U_{wall} = 0.7$ W/m²·K
- $A_{window} = 30$ m², $U_{window} = 5.0$ W/m²·K
- $A_{roof} = 250$ m², $U_{roof} = 0.4$ W/m²·K
- $\Delta T = 24 - 10 = 14$ K
- ACH = 6, $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1 — Transmission load through walls:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{wall} = U_{wall} \, A_{wall} \, \Delta T = 0.7 \times 200 \times 14
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{wall} = 1960 \; \text{W} = 1.96 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission load through windows:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{window} = U_{window} \, A_{window} \, \Delta T = 5.0 \times 30 \times 14
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{window} = 2100 \; \text{W} = 2.10 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmission load through roof:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{roof} = U_{roof} \, A_{roof} \, \Delta T = 0.4 \times 250 \times 14
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{roof} = 1400 \; \text{W} = 1.40 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Total transmission load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{trans} = 1.96 + 2.10 + 1.40 = 5.46 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Ventilation (Infiltration) Load:**

Mass flow rate of air entering the ward:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{V \times ACH}{3600} \times \rho_{air}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{600 \times 6}{3600} \times 1.2 = 2.0 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

Ventilation load:

$$
\dot{Q}_{vent} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T = 2.4 \times 1.005 \times 14
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{vent} = 33.77 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Heating Load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{trans} + \dot{Q}_{vent} = 5.46 + 33.77
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{Q}_{total} \approx 39.23 \; \text{kW}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — students frequently mix up W and kW; the answer must be in kW with units written clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the air density term** — ACH alone gives volume flow; you must multiply by $\rho$ to get mass flow.
> 3. **Skipping the $\Delta T$ calculation** — many students write $T_i$ and $T_o$ separately without subtracting.
> 4. **Ignoring infiltration vs total ACH** — in KTU papers, infiltration is often 1 ACH and total ventilation is given separately; students must read carefully.
> 5. **Round-off errors** — keep at least 2 decimal places for intermediate steps.
> 6. **Forgetting to add all three transmission components** — walls, windows, and roof must be summed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

### 🔹 Medical Vacuum & Oxygen
- [ ] Vacuum range: **−300 to −700 mmHg** for clinical suction.
- [ ] Liquid oxygen storage temperature: **−183 °C** at 1 atm.
- [ ] LOX gives **~860 L of gaseous O₂ per litre** of liquid.
- [ ] Medical oxygen purity: **≥ 99.0 % (USP)**.

### 🔹 Liquefaction Principle
- [ ] Joule–Thomson coefficient $\mu_{JT} = (\partial T / \partial P)_H$ — units **K/bar**.
- [ ] For air at 300 K, $\mu_{JT} \approx +0.25$ K/bar → air **cools** on expansion.
- [ ] Inversion temperature of air ≈ **621 K**.
- [ ] Critical temperature of O₂: **−118.57 °C**; N₂: **−146.96 °C**.
- [ ] O₂ boils first (higher BP) and is collected as the **liquid product**.

### 🔹 Simple Linde Process
- [ ] Core yield equation: $y = (h_{2''} - h_g) / (h_f - h_g)$.
- [ ] Ideal-gas approximation: $y = C_p (T_{2''} - T_g) / h_{fg}$.
- [ ] Compression work: $W_c = C_p T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$.
- [ ] Higher $P_2/P_1$ → higher $y$ but higher $W_c$ too.

### 🔹 Claude Cycle
- [ ] Adds an **isentropic expander** in parallel with the throttle.
- [ ] Produces more cooling per kg → higher yield.
- [ ] Used in large industrial oxygen plants (e.g., 50–500 TPD).

### 🔹 Hospital Air Quality
- [ ] OT: **21–24 °C**, **30–60 % RH**, **20–25 ACH**, **+12.5 Pa** (positive).
- [ ] Isolation: **−12.5 Pa** (negative).
- [ ] HEPA H13 filter: **≥ 99.97 %** at 0.3 μm.
- [ ] Microbial load OT: **< 35 CFU/m³**.

### 🔹 Heating Systems
- [ ] **LTHW (80–95 °C):** space heating via radiators / panels.
- [ ] **Steam (3–7 bar):** CSSD, laundry, kitchen, humidification.
- [ ] **Calorifier:** indirect DHW (no contamination).
- [ ] Heating load = transmission + ventilation + process + infiltration.
- [ ] Ventilation formula: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ with $\dot{m} = (V \cdot ACH / 3600) \cdot \rho$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC) - Basic Concepts and Standards in Different Hospital Areas - Refrigeration Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT501 - HOSPITAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC) - Basic Concepts and Standards in Different Hospital Areas - Refrigeration Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is HVAC in a Hospital Context?

**HVAC** stands for **Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning**. In a hospital environment, HVAC is not merely a comfort system; it is a critical **Infection Control Engineering System** that governs the indoor environmental quality (IEQ) of healthcare facilities. The system controls **temperature, humidity, air movement, air purity, and pressure differentials** across various zones to prevent the spread of airborne pathogens (e.g., *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Aspergillus* spores, SARS-CoV-2) and to protect immunocompromised patients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Hospital HVAC is an integrated engineering system that maintains prescribed temperature, relative humidity, air changes per hour (ACH), particulate filtration efficiency, and room pressure relationships in accordance with standards laid down by **ASHRAE 170 (Ventilation of Health Care Facilities)**, **NABH (National Accreditation Board for Hospitals)**, and **ISHRAE (Indian Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air Conditioning Engineers)**.

### 1.2 Subsystem Definitions

- **Heating**: The process of raising the dry-bulb temperature of indoor air using heat exchangers, boilers, or heat pumps.
- **Ventilation**: The intentional introduction of **outdoor (fresh) air** into a space and the removal of **stale indoor air**. It dilutes contaminants, CO₂, and bioaerosols.
- **Air Conditioning**: The simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and air distribution** to meet prescribed conditions for human occupancy or process requirements.
- **Refrigeration**: The branch of science dealing with the removal of heat from a substance at a lower temperature and rejecting it to a higher temperature sink — the fundamental thermodynamic backbone of all cooling-based HVAC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for HVAC parameters in hospitals:** **T-H-P-F-C** = **T**emperature, **H**umidity, **P**ressure differential, **F**iltration, **C**hanges of air per hour.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital as a Living Organism"

Imagine the hospital building is a **human body**:
- The **lungs** draw in fresh air (ventilation) and exhale stale air (exhaust).
- The **circulatory system (ducts)** distributes conditioned air to every "organ" (OT, ICU, ward).
- The **immune system (HEPA filters + UVGI)** traps and neutralizes harmful "pathogens" (particulates, microbes).
- The **kidneys (humidifiers/dehumidifiers)** regulate the moisture balance.
- The **heart (AHU/Chiller unit)** pumps the conditioned medium at the right pressure.

If any organ fails, the entire "body" (hospital) becomes susceptible to "infection" (HAI — Hospital Acquired Infection). HVAC is therefore the **lifeline of patient safety**.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard-Defining Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Hospital Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | T | 21 °C – 24 °C (OT: 18 °C – 22 °C) | ASHRAE 170 / NABH |
| Relative Humidity | RH | 30 % – 60 % (OT: 40 % – 60 %) | ASHRAE 170 |
| Air Changes per Hour | ACH | OT: 20, ICU: 12, Ward: 6 | ASHRAE 170 / FGI |
| Filtration Efficiency | — | OT: HEPA H13–H14 (≥ 99.97 % at 0.3 µm) | ISO 14644 |
| Positive Pressure | ΔP | OT: +12.5 Pa, Isolation: -12.5 Pa | CDC / WHO |
| Particulate Limit | PM | ISO Class 7 (OT), ISO Class 8 (Ward) | ISO 14644-1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HEPA Filter** = **H**igh-**E**fficiency **P**articulate **A**ir filter. A true HEPA filter must remove **≥ 99.97 %** of particles of diameter **0.3 µm** (the **Most Penetrating Particle Size, MPPS**).

---

## 1.5 Why Hospitals Need Special HVAC?

A residential AC only needs to provide *thermal comfort*. A hospital HVAC must additionally:

1. **Prevent cross-contamination** between sterile and non-sterile zones.
2. **Maintain unidirectional (laminar) airflow** in operating theatres.
3. **Control humidity** strictly to suppress microbial growth (bacteria thrive at RH > 60 %; viruses at RH < 40 %).
4. **Filter aspergillus spores** which are 2–3 µm and lethal to neutropenic patients.
5. **Maintain pressure cascades** in isolation rooms, OTs, and clean corridors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aspergillosis** is the most common airborne fungal infection in hospitals. It is acquired almost exclusively by **inhalation of spores**, which is why HEPA filtration in hematology-oncology wards is non-negotiable under NABH standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Hospital HVAC System — A Logic Decomposition

A complete hospital HVAC chain works in **five sequential stages**:

1. **Fresh Air Intake (FAI)**: Located ≥ 3 m above ground, ≥ 6 m from exhausts/boiler chimneys, equipped with a louver and a **G4 / MERV 7–8 pre-filter** to reject dust, leaves, and insects.
2. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)**: The "heart". It contains a **cooling coil, heating coil, humidifier, dehumidifier, fine filter (F7/F9), and a fan**. The AHU mixes return air (75–80 %) with fresh air (20–25 %) to conserve energy.
3. **Distribution Ductwork**: Galvanized Iron (GI) ducts with internal **liners** (fiberglass, closed-cell foam). Ducts are sealed to **Class C / B** as per SMACNA to prevent leakage.
4. **Terminal Devices**: **Grilles, diffusers, dampers, and VAV (Variable Air Volume) boxes** that deliver the conditioned air at the correct velocity, throw, and noise level (NC ≤ 35 for OTs).
5. **Exhaust System**: Independently ducted, with **back-draft dampers** to prevent reverse flow. Exhaust from OTs, isolation rooms, and mortuaries is **HEPA-filtered** before discharge.

---

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Hospital Ventilation

### A. Air Changes per Hour (ACH)
ACH is the **number of times** the entire volume of air in a room is replaced in **one hour**. It is the single most important design parameter for infection control.

### B. Pressure Differential (ΔP)
A **pressure cascade** is maintained so that air always flows *from clean to less-clean* zones. The pressure relationship is verified using a **Magnehelic gauge** or a **digital pressure monitor**.

- **Positive Pressure** rooms: OT, ICU, sterile storage → air flows **OUT** when door opens.
- **Negative Pressure** rooms: Airborne infection isolation (TB, COVID-19), bronchoscopy suite → air flows **IN** when door opens.

### C. Filtration Hierarchy (in sequence)
**G4 (Pre) → F7 (Fine) → F9 (Fine) → H13/H14 (HEPA) → optional ULPA**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ISO 16890 standard (post-2017) classifies filters by **ePM₁₀, ePM₂.₅, ePM₁₀** efficiency, while the older EN 779 used **G1–G4 (coarse) and F5–F9 (fine)**. KTU questions may still use the older convention; learn both.

---

## 2.3 Standards Governing Hospital HVAC — A Comparative Matrix

| Standard | Origin | Key Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **ASHRAE Standard 170** | USA (American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers) | Definitive ventilation rates, ACH, pressure, filtration by room class. |
| **NABH Standards (5th Edition)** | India (Quality Council of India) | Mandatory accreditation; references ASHRAE 170 / FGI / WHO. |
| **ASHRAE 62.1** | USA | Ventilation Rate Procedure (VRP) for acceptable IAQ. |
| **FGI Guidelines** | USA (Facility Guidelines Institute) | Healthcare facility design — used by AIA. |
| **WHO Infection Control Guidelines** | Global | Air sampling, isolation room design (epidemic settings). |
| **ISHRAE Handbook (India)** | India | Indian climatic data, psychometric applications. |
| **IS 3103 / IS 1391** | India (BIS) | Hospital building construction code. |
| **AERB Safety Code** | India (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board) | Special HVAC for Brachytherapy, OTs with radiological equipment. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Air changes per hour | h$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60}$ | Required supply airflow in CFM (V in m³, Q in m³/min → convert) | m³/h or L/s |
| 3 | $V_{\text{room}} = L \times B \times H$ | Room volume | m³ |
| 4 | $Q_s = 1.23 \times Q \times \Delta T$ | Sensible heat gain (Q in m³/s, ΔT in K) | kW |
| 5 | $Q_l = 3010 \times Q \times \Delta W$ | Latent heat gain (Q in m³/s, ΔW in kg water/kg dry air) | kW |
| 6 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Factor (ratio) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{\text{in}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance — refrigeration | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP limit (T in Kelvin) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $h = 1.005\,T + W(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | Moist air enthalpy (T in °C, W in kg/kg) | kJ/kg dry air |
| 10 | $P_v = P_s \times \dfrac{\text{RH}}{100}$ | Partial pressure of water vapour | kPa or Pa |
| 11 | $\text{tds} = 0.722 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | Specific humidity W (approx) | kg water/kg dry air |
| 12 | $P_{\text{static}} = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{velocity}}$ | Pressure in ducts | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For OTs and ICUs, design at **ASHRAE 62.1 default outdoor air rate OR the value from ASHRAE 170, whichever is higher**. NABH auditors routinely flag this mistake.

---

## 2.5 Why This Engineering Knowledge Matters

A civil/mechanical/biomedical engineer working in hospital infrastructure must:
- Specify **room class** to architects (e.g., "Class I OT — positive pressure, 20 ACH, HEPA H14").
- Validate **cleanroom classification** during commissioning using a **discrete particle counter** (ISO 14644-1).
- Conduct **airflow visualization (AFV)** studies using neutrally-buoyant fog or theatrical mist to demonstrate **laminar flow** in OTs.
- Audit **energy consumption** — HVAC accounts for **40–60 %** of a hospital's electricity bill, so **energy recovery wheels (ERVs)** are mandated by ECBC+ for new hospitals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Marks are awarded for *stating the standard value* (e.g., "OT requires 20 ACH as per ASHRAE 170") AND for *the engineering rationale* ("to dilute anaesthetic gases to below NIOSH exposure limits and to prevent microbial build-up"). One without the other is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air Changes per Hour (ACH) from First Principles

**Definition:** ACH is the volumetric flow rate of supply air (Q) divided by the room volume (V), with time scaling.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** Let the supply air volumetric flow rate be **Q** measured in **m³/h**.

**Step 2:** Let the room volume be **V** measured in **m³**.

**Step 3:** The time taken to supply one full room volume of air is:

$$t_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{V}{Q} \;\; [\text{hours}]$$

**Step 4:** The number of such cycles (i.e., complete air replacements) in one hour is:

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{1}{t_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{1}{V/Q} = \frac{Q}{V}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the fundamental relationship is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{ACH} = \frac{Q}{V}\;} \quad [\text{dimensionless, but expressed as h}^{-1}]$$

**Step 6:** If Q is measured in CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute) and V in cubic feet, the relationship becomes:

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q_{\text{CFM}} \times 60}{V_{\text{ft}^3}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Supply Airflow in m³/h

Starting from the ACH definition and inverting:

$$Q = \text{ACH} \times V$$

**Step 1:** Express the room volume as the product of its three internal dimensions:

$$V = L \times B \times H$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back into the ACH equation:

$$Q = \text{ACH} \times L \times B \times H$$

**Step 3:** This Q is in **m³/h** when dimensions are in metres and ACH is in h$^{-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> For ducts and AHU fan sizing, convert Q to **m³/s** by dividing by 3600, or to **CFM** by multiplying m³/h by **0.5886**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem (Model):** An Operation Theatre measures **8 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. Per ASHRAE 170, the design ACH is **20 h$^{-1}$**. Calculate:
(a) Required supply airflow in m³/h.
(b) Required supply airflow in CFM.
(c) Verify the time taken for one full air change.

**Solution:**

**(a) Calculation of room volume:**

$$V = L \times B \times H = 8 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 168 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**(b) Calculation of Q in m³/h:**

$$Q = \text{ACH} \times V = 20 \times 168 = 3360 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**(c) Conversion to m³/s (for AHU fan duty):**

$$Q = \frac{3360}{3600} = 0.9333 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(d) Conversion to CFM (1 m³/h = 0.5886 CFM):**

$$Q = 3360 \times 0.5886 = 1977.7 \;\; \text{CFM}$$

**(e) Time for one air change:**

$$t_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{168}{3360} = 0.05 \;\; \text{h} = 3 \;\; \text{minutes}$$

**Interpretation:** The entire volume of the OT air is replaced every 3 minutes. This high turnover is what dilutes anaesthetic gases, surgical smoke, and microbial aerosols to safe levels.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)

**Sensible heat** is the heat that changes **temperature only** (no phase change). **Latent heat** is the heat that changes **moisture content only** (phase change of water at constant temperature).

**Step 1:** Total cooling load is the sum of sensible and latent components:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_l$$

**Step 2:** The ratio of sensible to total is the Sensible Heat Factor:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_{\text{total}}} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$$

**Step 3:** If $Q_s = 1.23 \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T$ and $Q_l = 3010 \cdot Q \cdot \Delta W$, then:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{1.23 \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T}{1.23 \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T + 3010 \cdot Q \cdot \Delta W}$$

**Step 4:** Q cancels:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{1.23 \cdot \Delta T}{1.23 \cdot \Delta T + 3010 \cdot \Delta W}$$

**Step 5:** This is the canonical form used with a **psychrometric chart** to find the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** and the **effective coil bypass factor**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: COP of an Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle

The refrigeration cycle has four states: 1 (compressor inlet) → 2 (compressor outlet) → 3 (condenser outlet) → 4 (evaporator inlet).

**Step 1:** The refrigeration effect (heat absorbed in evaporator) per kg of refrigerant:

$$q_e = h_1 - h_4 \;\; [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

**Step 2:** The compressor work per kg of refrigerant:

$$w_{\text{in}} = h_2 - h_1 \;\; [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

**Step 3:** By definition, COP is the ratio of useful cooling to work input:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{q_e}{w_{\text{in}}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Step 4:** The **Carnot (theoretical maximum) COP** for a refrigerator operating between cold reservoir T_L and hot reservoir T_H (both in Kelvin) is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}$$

**Step 5:** For a typical OT supply condition of **T_L = 285 K (12 °C chilled water)** and ambient **T_H = 308 K (35 °C condenser)**:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{285}{308 - 285} = \frac{285}{23} = 12.39$$

A real chiller achieves COP ≈ 3.5–5.0 due to compressor irreversibilities, so the **second-law efficiency** is roughly **30–40 %**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Specific Humidity from Psychrometric Relationships

**Step 1:** By definition of specific humidity W (kg water vapour per kg dry air):

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

**Step 2:** The partial pressure of water vapour from relative humidity:

$$P_v = \text{RH} \times P_s(T)$$

**Step 3:** For the OT condition T = 21 °C, RH = 50 %: from steam tables, $P_s(21°\text{C}) = 2.487 \text{ kPa}$.

$$P_v = 0.50 \times 2.487 = 1.2435 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4:** With $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa:

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{1.2435}{101.325 - 1.2435} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.2435}{100.0815} = 0.00773 \;\; \text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 5:** This specific humidity, when plotted on the psychrometric chart, falls inside the **ASHRAE comfort zone** (T = 21 °C, RH = 50 %).

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Ventilation Standards by Hospital Area

| Hospital Area | Class (ISO 14644) | ACH (min) | Pressure | RH (%) | Filter Chain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **General OT (Class I)** | ISO 5 | **20** | **+12.5 Pa** | 40 – 60 | G4 + F9 + **H14 HEPA** |
| **ICU / CCU** | ISO 7 | 12 | + | 30 – 60 | G4 + F7 + F9 |
| **General Ward** | ISO 8 | **6** | Neutral | 30 – 60 | G4 + F7 |
| **Bronchoscopy Suite** | ISO 7 | 12 | **Negative (-12.5 Pa)** | 30 – 60 | G4 + F9 + H13 |
| **TB Isolation Room** | ISO 7 | **12** | **Negative (-12.5 Pa)** | 30 – 60 | G4 + F9 + **UVGI + H14** |
| **Bone Marrow Transplant** | ISO 5 | **≥ 20** | **+** | 40 – 60 | G4 + F9 + **H14 + LAF canopy** |
| **Pharmacy / CSSD Clean** | ISO 7 | 20 | + | 30 – 60 | G4 + F9 + H13 |
| **Mortuary / Autopsy** | ISO 8 | 12 | **Negative** | < 60 | G4 + F9 + H13 exhaust |
| **MRI Suite** | ISO 8 | 6 | Neutral | 30 – 60 | Non-magnetic ductwork; **no ferrous** parts |
| **Corridors** | ISO 9 | 2 – 4 | Flow toward less-clean | 30 – 60 | G4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite question:** "Justify the pressure and ACH requirement for a positive-pressure OT vs a negative-pressure isolation room." The *direction* of airflow is determined by the patient's condition: **protect patient from room** (OT, BMT) → positive; **protect room from patient** (TB, COVID) → negative.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `stage1`).
- No reserved words like `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone IDs.
- Node labels are plain uppercase text — no `**bold**`, no italics, no HTML tables inside quotes.

## 4.2 Diagram 1: Hospital HVAC Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[F1 OUTDOOR AIR INTAKE LOUVER 3M ABOVE GROUND] --> B[F2 PRE FILTER G4 MERV 8]
    B --> C[F3 MIXING CHAMBER 80 PERCENT RETURN PLUS 20 PERCENT FRESH]
    C --> D[F4 COOLING COIL CHILLED WATER 7 DEG C INLET]
    D --> E[F5 HEATING COIL HOT WATER REHEAT]
    E --> F[F6 HUMIDIFIER STEAM TYPE]
    F --> G[F7 FINE FILTER F7 MERV 13]
    G --> H[F8 FINE FILTER F9 MERV 14]
    H --> I[F9 HEPA FILTER H13 OR H14 OT AND BMT]
    I --> J[F10 SUPPLY FAN VARIABLE FREQUENCY]
    J --> K[F11 SUPPLY DUCTWORK GI CLASS B SEALED]
    K --> L[F12 TERMINAL DIFFUSERS LAMINAR FLOW FOR OT]
    L --> M[F13 CRITICAL ROOM OT OR ICU OR ISOLATION]
    M --> N[F14 RETURN GRILLE LOW LEVEL]
    N --> O[F15 RETURN DUCTWORK]
    O --> P[F16 RELIEF EXHAUST TO OUTSIDE]
    P --> Q[F17 EXHAUST FAN]
    Q --> R[F18 EXHAUST HEPA BEFORE DISCHARGE]
    O --> C
```

## 4.3 Diagram 2: Pressure Cascade Across Hospital Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zoneA[ZONE A CLEAN CORRIDOR POSITIVE PLUS 12.5 PA] --> zoneB[ZONE B OPERATING THEATRE POSITIVE PLUS 15 PA]
    zoneB --> zoneC[ZONE C SCRUB AREA NEUTRAL 0 PA]
    zoneC --> zoneD[ZONE D SOILED UTILITY NEGATIVE MINUS 5 PA]
    zoneD --> zoneE[ZONE E GENERAL CORRIDOR NEUTRAL]
    zoneE --> zoneF[ZONE F ISOLATION ROOM NEGATIVE MINUS 12.5 PA]
    zoneF --> zoneG[ZONE G ANTE ROOM NEUTRAL 0 PA]
    zoneG --> zoneE
    zoneA --> zoneE
```

## 4.4 Diagram 3: Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (Chiller Backbone)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1[STATE 1 SATURATED VAPOUR AT EVAPORATOR OUTLET] --> comp[COMPRESSOR WORK INPUT W IN]
    comp --> state2[STATE 2 SUPERHEATED VAPOUR HIGH PRESSURE HIGH TEMP]
    state2 --> cond[CONDENSER HEAT REJECTION Q C TO AMBIENT]
    cond --> state3[STATE 3 SATURATED LIQUID HIGH PRESSURE]
    state3 --> exp[EXPANSION VALVE THROTTLING PROCESS ISENTHALPIC]
    exp --> state4[STATE 4 LIQUID VAPOUR MIXTURE LOW PRESSURE]
    state4 --> evap[EVAPORATOR HEAT ABSORPTION Q E FROM CHILLED WATER]
    evap --> state1
```

## 4.5 Diagram 4: Decision Logic for Selecting HVAC Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START ROOM FUNCTION] --> q1{IS PATIENT IMMUNOCOMPROMISED}
    q1 -->|YES| pos[POSITIVE PRESSURE LAMINAR FLOW HEPA H14]
    q1 -->|NO| q2{IS PATIENT INFECTIOUS AIRBORNE}
    q2 -->|YES| neg[NEGATIVE PRESSURE 12 ACH EXHAUST HEPA H13]
    q2 -->|NO| q3{IS ROOM STERILE STORAGE OR PHARMACY}
    q3 -->|YES| pos2[POSITIVE PRESSURE 20 ACH HEPA H13]
    q3 -->|NO| gen[GENERAL WARD OR OPD 6 ACH NEUTRAL PRESSURE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU tip:** In viva questions, examiners may hand you a floor plan and ask you to **annotate the pressure relationships using arrows**. Use the above decision logic to justify each room.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, PCEBT501]**
**Define the term Air Changes Per Hour (ACH). Why is a high ACH specified for Operation Theatres as per ASHRAE 170?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**

ACH is the number of times the entire volume of air in a room is replaced in one hour, given by **$\text{ACH} = Q / V$** where Q is supply airflow and V is room volume. ASHRAE 170 mandates **20 ACH** in Operation Theatres to rapidly dilute **anaesthetic waste gases, surgical smoke, and airborne microbes**, thereby reducing the bioburden to levels safe for open surgical wounds. This high turnover prevents **Hospital-Acquired Infections (HAIs)** and maintains the **ISO Class 5 cleanliness** required for invasive procedures.

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — clinical justification.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCEBT501, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between positive and negative pressure rooms in a hospital. Give two examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):**

| Feature | Positive Pressure Room | Negative Pressure Room |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Protects the patient inside from outside contaminants | Protects the surroundings from the patient's infection |
| **Airflow direction at door** | Air flows **out** of the room | Air flows **into** the room |
| **Pressure relative to corridor** | **+12.5 Pa** (higher) | **-12.5 Pa** (lower) |
| **Examples** | 1. Operation Theatre, 2. Bone Marrow Transplant Unit | 1. TB Isolation Room, 2. COVID-19 ICU |
| **Exhaust filtration** | Filtered, may be recirculated | **HEPA-filtered and discharged outside**, no recirculation |

*(Examiner's allocation: 1.5 marks — conceptual difference with examples; 1.5 marks — engineering details.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice**. Both alternatives below are fully solved.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Refrigeration and Psychrometric Analysis** [KTU Model — ESE Pattern, Module 3, 14-Mark Standard]

**(a)** A hospital ward measures **10 m × 7 m × 3.2 m**. The design conditions are:
- Outdoor: 38 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT
- Indoor: 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH
- Sensible heat load inside = **8.5 kW**
- Latent heat load inside = **3.2 kW**
- Return air ratio = 80 %

Calculate:
1. The required **ACH** if ASHRAE recommends 6 for a general ward.
2. The **sensible heat factor (SHF)**.
3. The **mass flow rate of supply air** in kg/s, given the air enthalpy difference between outdoor and indoor states.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, explain the working of a **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** used as the chiller for a hospital's central AC plant. State the **refrigerants commonly used** and the **typical COP range** for hospital chillers.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question A**

**Part (a):** *Numerical Solution*

**Step 1 — Room volume:**

$$V = 10 \times 7 \times 3.2 = 224 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required supply airflow at 6 ACH:**

$$Q = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60} = \frac{6 \times 224}{60} = 22.4 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Converting to m³/h:

$$Q = 22.4 \times 60 = 1344 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.3733 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible Heat Factor:**

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l} = \frac{8.5}{8.5 + 3.2} = \frac{8.5}{11.7} = 0.7265 \;\;\text{(or 72.65 \%)}$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy of indoor air (24 °C, 50 % RH):**

From psychrometric chart, $h_i \approx 47.8$ kJ/kg dry air.

**Step 5 — Enthalpy of outdoor air (38 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT):**

From psychrometric chart, $h_o \approx 90.5$ kJ/kg dry air.

**Step 6 — Enthalpy difference (cooling coil must overcome):**

$$\Delta h = h_o - h_i = 90.5 - 47.8 = 42.7 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Mass flow rate of supply air (using total cooling load 11.7 kW = 11.7 kJ/s):**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\Delta h} = \frac{11.7}{42.7} = 0.274 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 8 — Verification using volumetric flow:**

Specific volume of air ≈ 0.87 m³/kg at 24 °C. So volumetric flow = $0.274 \times 0.87 = 0.238$ m³/s. The 80 % recirculation reduces fresh air load to **0.238 × 0.20 = 0.048 m³/s** of fresh intake, which is then conditioned.

> **Summary answer (a):** ACH = 6 h⁻¹, SHF = 0.7265, $\dot{m} = 0.274$ kg/s.

---

**Part (b):** *Descriptive Answer*

**Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Working:**

The VCRS operates on a **closed thermodynamic cycle** using a refrigerant (R-134a, R-407C, or R-410A in modern hospital chillers) that circulates through four main components:

1. **Compressor**: Low-pressure vapour refrigerant is compressed to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour. Work input = $w_{\text{in}} = h_2 - h_1$.
2. **Condenser**: The superheated vapour rejects heat $Q_c$ to the cooling medium (cooling tower water / ambient air) and condenses to a saturated liquid.
3. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)**: The high-pressure liquid passes through an expansion valve, undergoing an **isenthalpic** (constant-enthalpy) process. Pressure and temperature drop sharply; the refrigerant becomes a low-quality two-phase mixture.
4. **Evaporator**: The low-pressure mixture absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the chilled water circuit (typically 7 °C supply, 12 °C return), evaporating the liquid. This chilled water is then circulated to the AHU cooling coils in the hospital.

**The four states and processes:**
- 1 → 2 : Compression (work in)
- 2 → 3 : Condensation (heat out)
- 3 → 4 : Throttling (no work, no heat)
- 4 → 1 : Evaporation (heat in)

**COP of the cycle:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{\text{in}}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Commonly used refrigerants in hospital chillers:**
- **R-134a** (HFC, zero ODP, GWP 1430) — common in screw chillers.
- **R-407C** (HFC blend) — used in retrofit applications.
- **R-410A** (HFC blend) — common in DX systems.
- **R-1234ze** and **R-1234yf** — emerging low-GWP (< 1) alternatives under the Kigali Amendment / F-Gas regulation.
- **R-22 (HCFC)** — being phased out under the Montreal Protocol (Indian phase-out 2030–2040).

**Typical COP range:**
- **Water-cooled screw chiller**: COP 5.0 – 6.5
- **Air-cooled scroll chiller**: COP 2.8 – 3.6
- **Magnetic-bearing centrifugal chiller**: COP 6.0 – 7.0 (most efficient)
- **Inverter-driven (VFD) chiller** at part load: COP up to 8.0 (IPLV)

> *(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark — naming 4 components; 2 marks — explaining 4 processes with state points; 2 marks — listing refrigerants; 1 mark — COP formula; 1 mark — typical values.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Ventilation Standards for Hospital Areas** [KTU Model — ESE Pattern, Module 3, 14-Mark Standard]

**(a)** List the **ventilation and air-conditioning standards** applicable to hospital design in India. Compare **ASHRAE 170, NABH (5th Edition), and FGI** guidelines with respect to **Operating Theatre (OT) requirements** for: ACH, pressure relationship, filtration, and relative humidity.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the HVAC for a **Bone Marrow Transplant (BMT) Unit** of 6 rooms, each measuring **5 m × 4 m × 3 m**. The BMT unit requires **HEPA-filtered laminar airflow** with **positive pressure**. Calculate:
1. Total required supply airflow at 20 ACH.
2. The fan motor power if the total duct static pressure is 250 Pa and fan efficiency is 65 %.
3. Justify the use of **HEPA H14** over H13 for this application.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question B**

**Part (a):** *Standards Comparison*

**Standards applicable to hospital HVAC in India:**

1. **ASHRAE 170 — Ventilation of Health Care Facilities** (USA, but globally referenced).
2. **NABH 5th Edition Accreditation Standards** (India, mandatory for empanelment).
3. **ASHRAE 62.1 — Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality**.
4. **Facility Guidelines Institute (FGI) — Guidelines for Design and Construction of Hospitals**.
5. **WHO Infection Prevention and Control Guidelines**.
6. **ISHRAE Handbook (India-specific climatic data)**.
7. **BIS Standards IS 3103, IS 1391** (Indian hospital building codes).
8. **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 8 — Building Services**.

**Comparison for OT (Operating Theatre):**

| Parameter | **ASHRAE 170** | **NABH 5th Edition** | **FGI 2018** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ACH | 20 (Class A OT) | ≥ 20 (per ASHRAE) | 20 – 25 |
| Pressure | **+12.5 Pa** positive | Positive w.r.t. corridor | +0.01 to +0.02 in. w.c. (≈ 2.5 – 5 Pa) |
| Filtration | HEPA at supply | HEPA H13 minimum | HEPA 99.97 % |
| Relative Humidity | 20 – 60 % | 40 – 60 % | 20 – 60 % |
| Temperature | 20 – 24 °C | 21 – 24 °C | 20 – 24 °C |
| Airflow pattern | Unidirectional / laminar | Laminar (recommended) | Laminar or non-unidirectional |

**Key Insight:** NABH references ASHRAE 170 by adoption, making ASHRAE the de-facto standard. FGI gives **lower pressure differentials** which is one of the criticisms of FGI in India — NABH adopts the stricter ASHRAE values.

> *(Examiner's allocation: 2 marks — naming standards; 3 marks — comparative table; 2 marks — critical commentary.)*

---

**Part (b):** *Numerical Design*

**Given:** 6 rooms, each 5 m × 4 m × 3 m; ACH = 20; fan static = 250 Pa; fan efficiency = 65 %.

**Step 1 — Volume of one room:**

$$V_1 = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Supply airflow per room:**

$$Q_1 = \text{ACH} \times V_1 = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total airflow for 6 rooms:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 1200 = 7200 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³/s for fan power calculation:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{7200}{3600} = 2.0 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical hydraulic power:**

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = Q \times \Delta P = 2.0 \times 250 = 500 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Fan shaft power (accounting for fan efficiency η_fan = 0.65):**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{\eta_{\text{fan}}} = \frac{500}{0.65} = 769.23 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 7 — Motor power (accounting for motor efficiency η_motor = 0.90, typical):**

$$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_{\text{motor}}} = \frac{769.23}{0.90} = 854.7 \;\; \text{W} \approx 0.86 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> **Recommended motor rating:** Select a **1.1 kW** motor (next standard size up, with service factor).

**Step 8 — Justification of HEPA H14 over H13 for BMT:**

| Filter Class | **Minimum Efficiency at MPPS (0.3 µm)** | Application |
|---|---|---|
| H13 | ≥ **99.95 %** | Standard HEPA — general OTs, pharmacies |
| H14 | ≥ **99.995 %** | BMT, organ transplant, immunocompromised wards |

**Justification (engineering + clinical):**
1. BMT patients have **absolute neutropenia** (neutrophil count < 500/µL) for weeks post-transplant.
2. Inhalation of even **1 Aspergillus spore** (2–3 µm) can cause **Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis (IPA)**, mortality > 50 %.
3. H14 removes **10× more particles** at the MPPS than H13, which is critical when the infectious dose is so low.
4. H14 also provides a larger **safety margin** against filter degradation, leaks, and shedding of media over the 3–5 year service life.
5. WHO and ECIL (European Conference on Infections in Leukaemia) recommend H14-grade filtration for **allogeneic BMT units**.

> *(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark — V₁; 1 mark — Q_total; 1 mark — conversion; 1 mark — P_hyd; 1 mark — P_shaft; 1 mark — P_motor; 1 mark — H14 justification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HEPA grades** — writing "H14 removes 99.97 %" is wrong. That is H13. H14 = **99.995 %**. Examiners will deduct **½ to 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Mixing up ASHRAE 62.1 and 170** — 62.1 is generic ventilation; 170 is healthcare-specific. Many students quote 62.1 for OT.
> 3. **Wrong sign on negative pressure rooms** — saying "positive pressure for TB isolation" is a **clinical disaster** and will cost **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting 80/20 mixing** — AHU uses 80 % return + 20 % fresh air. Omitting this means energy calculations are wrong by **5×**.
> 5. **SI unit errors** — using CFM when SI is expected (or vice versa) in a 14-mark numerical. Always show the conversion step.
> 6. **No diagram in descriptive answers** — Part B descriptive questions **require a labelled diagram** for full marks. A sketch earns 1–2 extra marks; a well-labelled schematic earns 2–3.
> 7. **Skipping the standard reference** — Stating "20 ACH" without "per ASHRAE 170 / NABH" is treated as an incomplete answer. **Always cite the standard.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **HVAC = Heating + Ventilation + Air Conditioning**; in hospitals, it is an infection-control system, not a comfort system.
- **ASHRAE 170** is the primary standard; **NABH 5th Edition** adopts it in India; **WHO** is the global public-health reference.
- **OT (Class A)**: 20 ACH, +12.5 Pa, 40–60 % RH, HEPA H13/H14, 21–24 °C, ISO Class 5.
- **General Ward**: 6 ACH, neutral pressure, 30–60 % RH, F7/F9 filtration.
- **TB / COVID Isolation**: 12 ACH, **–12.5 Pa**, exhaust HEPA-filtered to atmosphere, **no recirculation**.
- **BMT Unit**: ≥ 20 ACH, **HEPA H14 mandatory**, +12.5 Pa, 40–60 % RH, laminar flow canopy.
- **Formulas to memorise**: $\text{ACH} = Q/V$; $Q = \text{ACH} \times L \times B \times H$; $\text{SHF} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$; $\text{COP} = Q_e / W_{\text{in}}$; $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.
- **HEPA = 99.97 % at 0.3 µm**; **H14 = 99.995 %**; ULPA ≥ 99.9995 %.
- **Refrigeration cycle states**: 1→2 compression, 2→3 condensation, 3→4 throttling (isenthalpic), 4→1 evaporation.
- **Common refrigerants**: R-134a (HFC), R-407C, R-410A; **R-22 is being phased out** under the Montreal Protocol; low-GWP alternatives: R-1234ze, R-1234yf.
- **Typical chiller COP**: 3.0 – 6.5 (real); up to 12+ (Carnot limit).
- **HVAC accounts for 40–60 %** of hospital electricity → use **VFDs, EC fans, ERVs (energy recovery wheels)**.
- **Airflow visualization (AFV)** with theatre fog is a NABH commissioning requirement for every OT.
- **Filters in series**: G4 → F7 → F9 → H13/H14. Never replace a stage; it compromises downstream filters.
- **Pressure cascade mnemonic**: **"OUT means Open To corridor for OT"** (positive) — **"IN means Is oNegate for isolation"** (negative).
- **KTU mark-boosters**: always cite the standard; always draw a labelled diagram in Part B; always show unit conversions; always discuss clinical rationale (HAI prevention) and not just engineering.

> **Final memory line for viva:** *"Hospital HVAC is a sterile, pressure-controlled, HEPA-filtered lung that breathes the building twenty times an hour — it is the silent, indispensable first line of defence against infection."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Changes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT501 - HOSPITAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Changes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Air Change (per Hour, ACH)** is defined as the volume of supply air introduced into (or removed from) a conditioned space in one hour, expressed as a multiple of the **net internal volume** of that space. It is the single most fundamental design metric used by hospital HVAC engineers to control microbial concentration, dilution of anaesthetic gases, removal of thermal/chemical loads, and maintenance of the prescribed **pressure cascade** between adjacent clinical zones.

Mathematically expressed by ISHRAE, ASHRAE 170, and FGI Guidelines (all standard reference frameworks adopted in KTU modules):

$$
\text{ACH} \;=\; \frac{Q \;\times\; 3600}{V}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = supply / exhaust air volume flow rate in **m³/s**
- $V$ = internal volume of the room in **m³**
- The factor **3600** converts seconds → hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEBT501, Module 3):** Air Changes form the backbone of Hospital Ventilation Design. The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to (a) compute ACH numerically, (b) interpret recommended values for each hospital zone, and (c) justify pressure relationships (positive / negative / neutral).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs of a Hospital Room"

Imagine a glass box (your room) containing stale, contaminated air. Now imagine a pair of mechanical "lungs" — the AHU (Air Handling Unit) — that *inhales* a measured quantity of clean, filtered air and *exhales* the contaminated air every cycle.

- **One complete breath** = the box's air being replaced **once**.
- **ACH = 20** means the room "breathes" **twenty full lungfuls every hour**.

If the patient in the room is shedding bacteria or virus-laden aerosols (like in an **Infectious Disease Ward**), the higher the ACH, the **faster the pathogen concentration falls**. This is exactly why Operation Theatres demand **20–25 ACH** while a corridor only needs **2–4 ACH** — the infection-risk weight is asymmetric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three constants/metrics you must memorise for KTU exams:**
> - Standard temperature for ventilation math: **21 °C** (294 K) for OT-class spaces.
> - Standard pressure differential across hospital doors: **2.5 Pa** (ASHRAE) or **12.5 Pa** (FGI for airborne infection isolation).
> - Filtration benchmark: **HEPA filter** efficiency ≥ **99.97 %** at **0.3 µm** (MPPS).

### 1.3 Categories of Air Change in Hospital Engineering

| Symbol | Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $ACH_{supply}$ | Supply ACH | Total supply air volume per hour ÷ room volume. |
| $ACH_{exhaust}$ | Exhaust ACH | Total exhaust air volume per hour ÷ room volume. |
| $ACH_{OA}$ | Outside-Air ACH | Fresh (outdoor) air volume per hour ÷ room volume. |
| $\Delta P$ | Pressure ACH | Same volume flows but maintained against a pressure barrier. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the ACH Equation

**Step 1 — Quantify the room volume.**
For a rectangular room: $V = L \times B \times H$. Subtract the volume of permanent fixtures (OT table, beds, cabinets) only if explicitly stated in the problem; otherwise treat $V$ as the *gross internal volume*.

**Step 2 — Determine the airflow rate $Q$.**
This is delivered by the AHU blower and measured (or specified) at the supply diffuser grille after accounting for duct leakage (~5–10 %) and filter pressure drop.

**Step 3 — Convert the time base.**
Engineers specify blowers in **m³/s** (SI) or **CFM** (Cubic Feet per Minute, used in many imported equipment datasheets). Conversion factor: **1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s**.

**Step 4 — Apply the KTU-formula.**

$$
\text{ACH} \;=\; \frac{Q_{supply} \;[\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]}{V \;[\text{m}^3]}
$$

If $Q$ is given in m³/s, multiply by **3600** before dividing.

**Step 5 — Sanity-check against hospital code.**
Compare the computed value against **ASHRAE 170** / **ISHRAE** minimums. If lower → **under-design** (mark loss). If higher → **over-design** (acceptable but uneconomical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** The ACH value directly drives (a) blower sizing, (b) duct cross-section, (c) filter bank cost, and (d) annual energy consumption. An OT designed at 25 ACH instead of 20 ACH consumes ~25 % more fan energy for the *same* room.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \cdot 3600}{V}$ | Air Changes per Hour | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | Room internal volume | m³ |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{ACH} \cdot V}{3600}$ | Required supply flow | m³/s |
| 4 | $1\;\text{CFM} = 0.0004719\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Imperial→SI conversion | m³/s |
| 5 | $1\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2118.88\;\text{CFM}$ | SI→Imperial conversion | CFM |
| 6 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{3600}{\text{ACH}}$ | Mean residence time of an air parcel | seconds |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{room} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | Pressure cascade sign convention | Pa |
| 8 | $C/C_0 = e^{-\text{ACH} \cdot t / 3600}$ | Contaminant decay (well-mixed) | fraction |
| 9 | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Equivalent first-order decay | count/m³ |

### 2.3 Recommended ACH by Hospital Zone (ASHRAE 170 / ISHRAE Alignment)

| Zone | Min ACH | Pressure Relation | Filter Level |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Class I / II Operation Theatre | **20 – 25** | Positive (+12.5 Pa) | HEPA |
| Cardiac / Neuro OT | **25** | Positive (+12.5 Pa) | HEPA |
| ICU / CCU | **12 – 15** | Positive (+2.5 Pa) | HEPA 95 % |
| Airborne Infection Isolation (AII) | **12** | **Negative** (−12.5 Pa) | HEPA on exhaust |
| Protective Environment (Bone-marrow) | **12** | Positive (+12.5 Pa) | HEPA |
| Post-Op Recovery | **6 – 8** | Neutral | 90 % |
| General Ward | **4 – 6** | Neutral | 30 % |
| Emergency Department | **12 – 15** | Neutral | 90 % |
| Pharmacy / Clean Store | **4** | Positive (+2.5 Pa) | 90 % |
| Corridors (patient) | **2 – 4** | Neutral | 30 % |
| Mortuary / Autopsy | **12** | Negative (−2.5 Pa) | 95 % exhaust |
| Dirty Utility / Soiled Linen | **10** | Negative (−2.5 Pa) | 95 % exhaust |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Blower selection:** Fan static pressure rating (typically 500–1500 Pa for hospital AHUs) must overcome filter banks, duct friction, and the intended $\Delta P$.
- **Energy audit:** A 200-bed hospital spends **40–60 %** of its electricity on HVAC. Optimising ACH to code-minimum (not code-maximum) yields payback periods under **3 years**.
- **Infection control auditing:** NABH accreditation (mandatory for Indian hospitals) directly inspects ACH compliance using a balometer or anemometer at grille level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental ACH Equation

**Starting premise.** Suppose a perfectly-mixed room of volume $V$ m³ receives a constant supply airflow $Q$ m³/s. The exhaust airflow is identically $Q$ (steady-state mass balance). Let $C(t)$ denote the contaminant concentration at time $t$.

**Mass balance on contaminant:**

$$
V \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} \;=\; Q \cdot C_{in} \;-\; Q \cdot C(t) \;-\; k \cdot V \cdot C(t)
$$

The first term is contaminant entering with supply air, the second is contaminant leaving with exhaust, and the third is first-order decay (e.g., settling, biological inactivation) with rate constant $k$ [s⁻¹].

**Assuming supply air is clean** ($C_{in} = 0$) and neglecting decay ($k = 0$):

$$
V \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} \;=\; -\,Q \cdot C(t)
$$

**Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\,\frac{Q}{V}\, dt
$$

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\,\frac{Q}{V} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) \;=\; -\,\frac{Q}{V}\, t
$$

**Exponentiate and substitute ACH:**

$$
C(t) \;=\; C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q\, t}{V}\right) \;=\; C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\text{ACH}\cdot t}{3600}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical "well-mixed room decay" equation** the KTU paper-setter loves. After **one air change** ($t = 3600 / \text{ACH}$), concentration drops to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of original. After **five air changes**, only $0.67\%$ remains (i.e., **5 ACH-cycles achieve 99.33 % removal** in clean-up theory).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Basic ACH Computation

**Problem.** An ICU room measures 6 m × 5 m × 3.2 m (L × B × H). The AHU supplies 0.40 m³/s of filtered air. Calculate: (a) the room volume, (b) the ACH, and (c) the residence time.

**(a) Volume:**

$$
V \;=\; 6 \times 5 \times 3.2 \;=\; 96 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**(b) Air Changes per Hour:**

$$
\text{ACH} \;=\; \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} \;=\; \frac{0.40 \times 3600}{96} \;=\; \frac{1440}{96} \;=\; 15
$$

**(c) Mean residence time:**

$$
t_{res} \;=\; \frac{3600}{\text{ACH}} \;=\; \frac{3600}{15} \;=\; 240 \;\text{seconds} \;=\; 4 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Interpretation.** 15 ACH meets the ASHRAE-170 ICU requirement (12–15 ACH). Air parcels dwell ~4 minutes before being exhausted.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Reverse Design (Find Q for a Given ACH)

**Problem.** A Class-I Operation Theatre must achieve **25 ACH**. Room dimensions: 7 m × 6 m × 3.5 m. Compute the required blower flow rate in **m³/s** and in **CFM**.

**Volume:**

$$
V \;=\; 7 \times 6 \times 3.5 \;=\; 147 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Required flow in m³/hr:**

$$
Q \;=\; \text{ACH} \cdot V \;=\; 25 \times 147 \;=\; 3675 \;\text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Convert to m³/s:**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{3675}{3600} \;=\; 1.0208 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Convert to CFM:**

$$
Q \;=\; 1.0208 \times 2118.88 \;\approx\; 2162.7 \;\text{CFM}
$$

**With 10 % leakage + filter degradation allowance, specify a fan rated at ~1.15 m³/s (~2435 CFM).**

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Contaminant Decay in OT

**Problem.** During a procedure, 1 µg/m³ of anaesthetic vapour leaks into a 147 m³ OT supplied at 25 ACH. Assuming well-mixed decay, what is the concentration after **15 minutes**?

**Decay equation:**

$$
C(t) \;=\; C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\text{ACH}\cdot t}{3600}\right)
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
C(900\;\text{s}) \;=\; 1.0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{25 \times 900}{3600}\right) \;=\; \exp(-6.25)
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
C(900\;\text{s}) \;=\; 0.00193 \;\text{µg/m}^3
$$

**Removal efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \left(1 - 0.00193\right) \times 100\% \;\approx\; 99.81\%
$$

This justifies the 25-ACH mandate — even after only 15 minutes, anaesthetic vapour falls to negligible trace levels.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Answers

| Given | Find | First Formula to Write |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q$ in m³/s, room size | ACH | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \cdot 3600}{V}$ |
| ACH and room size | $Q$ in m³/s | $Q = \dfrac{\text{ACH} \cdot V}{3600}$ |
| $Q$ in CFM, room in ft³ | ACH (imperial) | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{Q \cdot 60}{V}$ |
| $C_0$ and $t$, known ACH | $C(t)$ | $C(t) = C_0 e^{-\text{ACH} \cdot t / 3600}$ |
| Two adjacent rooms, $P_1, P_2$ | Direction of leakage | $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ |
| Fan power, flow, $\Delta P_{tot}$ | Fan efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{P_{elec}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Air-Change Functional Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROOM VOLUME V m3"] --> B["AHU SUPPLY BLOWER"]
    B --> C["PRE FILTER"]
    C --> D["COOLING COIL"]
    D --> E["HEPA FILTER"]
    E --> F["SUPPLY DIFFUSER GRILLE"]
    F --> G["CLINICAL ZONE OT ICU WARD"]
    G --> H["RETURN AIR GRILLE"]
    H --> I["RETURN FAN"]
    I --> J["EXHAUST HEPA OR LOUVER"]
    J --> K["OUTSIDE AIR DUMP"]
    G --> L["PRESSURE SENSOR DELTA P MONITOR"]
    L --> M["CONTROLLER MODULATES BLOWER SPEED"]
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting Pressure Relation (Positive / Negative / Neutral)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START CHOOSE ZONE TYPE"] --> Q1["IS THE PATIENT IMMUNO COMPROMISED"]
    Q1 -- YES --> PP["PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT POSITIVE 12.5 Pa"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2["IS THE PATIENT INFECTIOUS AIRBORNE"]
    Q2 -- YES --> NN["AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION NEGATIVE 12.5 Pa"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3["IS THE ROOM A STERILE PROCEDURE AREA"]
    Q3 -- YES --> POS["POSITIVE PRESSURE 2.5 TO 12.5 Pa"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4["IS THE ROOM A SOILED DIRTY UTILITY"]
    Q4 -- YES --> NEG["NEGATIVE PRESSURE 2.5 Pa"]
    Q4 -- NO --> NEU["NEUTRAL PRESSURE WARD CORRIDOR OFFICE"]
```

### 4.3 ACH Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT ROOM DIMENSIONS L B H"] --> B["COMPUTE V = L times B times H"]
    C["INPUT FAN FLOW Q m3 per s"] --> D["APPLY ACH = Q times 3600 / V"]
    B --> D
    D --> E{"ACH MEETS CODE MINIMUM"}
    E -- YES --> F["ACCEPT DESIGN SIZE DUCT GRILLE"]
    E -- NO --> G["ITERATE INCREASE FAN OR REDUCE V"]
    G --> D
    F --> H["OUTPUT FINAL ACH AND Q"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
Define the term **Air Change per Hour (ACH)** as applied to hospital ventilation. State the governing equation and its units.

**Model Answer:**
Air Change per Hour (ACH) is defined as the ratio of the volume of air supplied to (or exhausted from) a conditioned space in one hour to the net internal volume of that space.

$$
\text{ACH} \;=\; \frac{Q \times 3600}{V}
$$

Where $Q$ is in m³/s and $V$ in m³. ACH is dimensionless. It quantifies how many times the entire air content of the room is replenished per hour, directly governing contaminant dilution, thermal-load removal, and pressure-cascade maintenance in clinical zones. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
Why does a Class-I Operation Theatre require **20–25 ACH** while a patient corridor requires only **2–4 ACH**? Justify with reference to infection-risk and pressure cascades.

**Model Answer:**
Operation Theatres host open surgical wounds vulnerable to airborne microbial contamination (e.g., skin squames, droplet nuclei). A high ACH rapidly dilutes and removes these particles, with HEPA filtration ensuring near-zero microbial counts downstream. The positive pressure (~+12.5 Pa) prevents corridor air from infiltrating the OT when the door opens. Conversely, corridors carry diluted contaminants and no open wounds; lower ACH suffices because (a) infection risk is low, (b) energy economy is prioritised, and (c) the corridor typically sits at neutral pressure to avoid disturbing adjacent zones' pressure cascades. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A Class-I Operation Theatre has internal dimensions **7 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. The air-handling unit delivers **1.10 m³/s** of supply air and the room is to be maintained at **+12.5 Pa** positive pressure relative to the corridor. Calculate:
(i) The room volume.
(ii) The Air Changes per Hour (ACH).
(iii) The mean residence time of an air parcel inside the room.
Comment on whether the design satisfies ASHRAE-170 recommendations.

**(b)** During an equipment sterilisation cycle, **500 µg/m³** of ethylene-oxide vapour leaks into the OT. Using the well-mixed decay model, determine the time required for the concentration to fall below the occupational exposure limit of **1 µg/m³**. Comment on the engineering implications.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** (i) Room volume:

$$
V = 7 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 147 \;\text{m}^3
$$

(ii) Air changes per hour:

$$
\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} = \frac{1.10 \times 3600}{147} = \frac{3960}{147} \approx 26.94
$$

(iii) Mean residence time:

$$
t_{res} = \frac{3600}{\text{ACH}} = \frac{3600}{26.94} \approx 133.6 \;\text{s} \approx 2 \;\text{min}\;14\;\text{s}
$$

**Comment:** ACH ≈ 27 satisfies the ASHRAE-170 Class-I OT requirement (≥ 20 ACH, ideally 25). The +12.5 Pa positive pressure ensures air flows outward whenever the door opens, preventing corridor-to-OT contamination. The design is therefore **compliant and conservative**, providing a safety margin against filter loading. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Decay equation (well-mixed, clean supply):

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\text{ACH}\cdot t}{3600}\right)
$$

Substitute $C(t) = 1\;\mu g/m^3$, $C_0 = 500\;\mu g/m^3$, ACH = 26.94:

$$
\frac{1}{500} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{26.94 \cdot t}{3600}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(0.002) = -\frac{26.94 \cdot t}{3600}
$$

$$
-6.2146 = -\frac{26.94 \cdot t}{3600}
$$

$$
t = \frac{6.2146 \times 3600}{26.94} \approx 830.4 \;\text{s} \approx 13.85 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- ~**14 minutes** of post-leak scavenging is required before staff may re-enter without respiratory protection.
- The OT should have a **delayed re-entry interlock** and an audible alarm tied to the AHU controls.
- Ethylene-oxide is carcinogenic; a redundant **gas-detection sensor** with automatic AHU ramp-up to maximum ACH (30+) is recommended.
- Annual preventive maintenance must verify HEPA integrity and blower performance, since filter loading reduces effective ACH by 5–15 %. *(7 marks)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **positive-pressure** and **negative-pressure** isolation rooms. For each, give **two example diseases**, the recommended **ACH value**, the **pressure differential**, and the **exhaust filtration** requirement.

**(b)** A 5 m × 4 m × 3 m general ward is to be upgraded to a **12-ACH** isolation standard. Calculate:
(i) The required supply flow in **m³/hr** and **CFM**.
(ii) The new mean residence time.
(iii) The required fan motor power assuming a total external static pressure (ESP) of **750 Pa** and fan efficiency of **65 %**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** Definitions and parameters:

| Parameter | Positive-Pressure Room | Negative-Pressure Room |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | $P_{room} > P_{corridor}$ to keep contaminants out. | $P_{room} < P_{corridor}$ to keep contaminants in. |
| Typical Patients | Immunocompromised (e.g., bone-marrow transplant, severe burns, leukaemia induction) | Infectious airborne (e.g., pulmonary TB, measles, COVID-19, varicella) |
| ACH | ≥ 12 | ≥ 12 |
| Pressure $\Delta P$ | +12.5 Pa | −12.5 Pa |
| Exhaust filter | Standard, discharged to outside | HEPA-filtered exhaust |

*(7 marks)*

**(b)** (i) Volume and supply flow:

$$
V = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60 \;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
Q = \text{ACH} \cdot V = 12 \times 60 = 720 \;\text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

In CFM:

$$
Q = \frac{720}{2118.88 / 3600} \quad \text{or directly:} \quad Q_{CFM} = \frac{720}{1.699} \approx 423.8 \;\text{CFM}
$$

(ii) Mean residence time:

$$
t_{res} = \frac{3600}{12} = 300 \;\text{s} = 5 \;\text{minutes}
$$

(iii) Fan motor power (shaft / input relation):

$$
P_{shaft} = Q \cdot \Delta P_{tot}
$$

With $Q$ in m³/s: $Q = 720 / 3600 = 0.20\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$

$$
P_{shaft} = 0.20 \times 750 = 150 \;\text{W}
$$

With fan efficiency 65 %:

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta} = \frac{150}{0.65} \approx 230.8 \;\text{W}
$$

Specify a **0.25 kW (¼ HP)** standard motor with a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) for night setback.

*(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Air-Change questions (KTU 2024 Board Examiner pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3600 conversion factor** when $Q$ is supplied in m³/s. This alone costs **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** by writing $V/Q$ instead of $Q/V$. Always re-derive symbolically before substituting.
> 3. **Ignoring units of CFM vs m³/s.** Indian exam problems may give one in each system; use **1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s**.
> 4. **Not commenting on code-compliance.** A numerical answer without a sentence on ASHRAE-170 / NABH compliance loses the **1-mark "design-comment" tag**.
> 5. **Treating pressure direction carelessly.** Always state *"positive pressure relative to corridor"* with sign — examiners mark the **sign** strictly.
> 6. **Skipping the leakage/derating allowance** in real-design problems. Add ~10 % when specifying fan rating.
> 7. **In decay problems, using wrong ACH-vs-t unit match.** ACH is per hour; if $t$ is in seconds, multiply ACH by $t$ and divide by 3600. Common blunder worth **3 marks** lost.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACH** is a dimensionless ratio of hourly supply volume to room volume; it governs **infection control, thermal-load removal, and pressure cascade integrity** in hospitals.
- The governing equation $\text{ACH} = (Q \times 3600)/V$ must be written **with units** in the KTU answer script.
- **Standard ACH values** to memorise: OT 20–25, ICU 12–15, AII/PE 12, general ward 4–6, corridor 2–4.
- **Positive pressure** for protective environments; **negative pressure** for infectious isolation; **12.5 Pa** differential per ASHRAE-170.
- **Mean residence time** $= 3600 / \text{ACH}$ seconds; shorter for higher-ACH zones.
- **Contaminant decay** in a well-mixed room follows $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\text{ACH}\cdot t / 3600}$. Five air-change cycles yield ~99.33 % removal.
- **Conversion constants**: 1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s; 1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM; 1 m³/hr = 0.5886 CFM (round memorise).
- **Fan power** $P_{motor} = (Q \cdot \Delta P) / \eta$; account for **5–10 % leakage** and **filter degradation** when sizing.
- **Filter hierarchy**: Pre-filter (G4, ≥ 90 % at 10 µm) → Fine filter (F7, ≥ 95 % at 1 µm) → HEPA (H13/H14, ≥ 99.97 % at 0.3 µm).
- **Energy implication**: every additional ACH in a 150 m³ OT costs approximately **1.5–2 kWh/day** in fan energy — design to **code minimum, not code maximum**.
- Always conclude a KTU numerical answer with a **design-justification sentence** referencing ASHRAE-170, ISHRAE, or NABH.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filtering and Sterility, Sterilization Systems in Hospitals: Principles and Techniques of Sterilization - Steam and EO Sterilization, Autoclaves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT501 - HOSPITAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Filtering and Sterility, Sterilization Systems in Hospitals: Principles and Techniques of Sterilization - Steam and EO Sterilization, Autoclaves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 | Sterilization Systems in Hospitals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Sterilization?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sterilization** is a validated process used to render a product free from viable microorganisms, including bacterial spores, fungi, viruses, and prions. The theoretical probability of a viable organism surviving the process is less than **one in a million** (i.e., Sterility Assurance Level, **SAL = 10⁻⁶**).

In the KTU 2024 Hospital Engineering context, sterilization is the **terminal process** applied to medical devices, surgical instruments, linens, fluids, and pharmaceutical products to make them safe for direct patient contact or invasive use.

### 1.2 What is Sterility?

> [!NOTE]
> **Sterility** is the absolute state of being free from all living microorganisms. A sterile item is one that has undergone a validated sterilization process and is maintained in a state that prevents recontamination.

### 1.3 What is Filtering in the Hospital Context?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filtering (Filtration Sterilization)** is a physical method of removing microorganisms from fluids (liquids or gases) by passing them through a filter medium with pores small enough to retain bacteria and spores, without exposing the fluid to heat or chemicals. Filters with **0.22 µm** pore size are the pharmaceutical industry standard for bacterial removal.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Making It Click

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Sterilization vs. Cleaning**
> Think of your kitchen countertop. Wiping it with a damp cloth (cleaning) removes visible dirt. Spraying it with antiseptic (disinfection) kills most germs. But placing a surgical instrument in an autoclave is like putting it inside a "pressure cooker of death" for microbes — every single organism, even the toughest dormant spores, is irreversibly destroyed. That is sterilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Filtration**
> Imagine pouring muddy water through a fine sieve into a clean glass. The dirt stays on top, the clear water drips through. A 0.22 µm filter does the same thing to a drug solution — bacteria and spores are too large to pass through and are trapped, while the liquid medication flows through clean and sterile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — EO Gas**
> Picture an invisible, aggressive "chemical fog" seeping into every crack of a delicate plastic syringe. The fog is Ethylene Oxide (EO). It does not damage the plastic, but it chemically destroys the DNA and proteins of any microbe hiding inside the syringe's lumen or seams. This is the only practical way to sterilize heat-sensitive plastics and electronics.

### 1.5 Classification of Sterilization Methods (KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Methods | Primary Application |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **Physical** | Steam (Autoclave), Dry Heat, Filtration, Radiation (Gamma/E-beam) | Heat-resistant metal instruments, fluids, drugs |
| **Chemical** | Ethylene Oxide (EO), Formaldehyde, Hydrogen Peroxide Plasma, Glutaraldehyde | Heat-sensitive plastics, rubber, electronics |
| **Combined** | Steam + Chemical indicators, Plasma + H₂O₂ vapor | Modern integrated CSSD cycles |

### 1.6 Key Standard Metrics in Sterilization

- **SAL (Sterility Assurance Level)** = **10⁻⁶** (probability of one survivor per million units)
- **D-value** = Decimal Reduction Time (time to kill 90% of a microbial population at a given temperature)
- **Z-value** = Temperature change required to change D-value by a factor of 10
- **F-value** = Equivalent sterilization time at a reference temperature
- **Steam Quality** must meet **EN 285** standard: dryness fraction ≥ 0.95, non-condensable gases < 3.5%
- **EO Residual Limits** per **ISO 10993-7**: ≤ 4 mg/24 hrs average for devices in contact with blood/bone

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Sterilization Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that any sterilization process rests on four engineering parameters that can be independently controlled:

1. **Agent** — What is doing the killing? (Steam, EO gas, HEPA-filtered air)
2. **Concentration / Pressure** — How much agent is present?
3. **Temperature** — What is the thermodynamic state?
4. **Time (Exposure Duration)** — How long is the agent in contact?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"4 × 4 rule"** of sterilization: To kill microbes, you must control 4 parameters (agent, concentration, temperature, time) and verify them with 4 monitoring tools (physical gauges, chemical indicators, biological indicators, parametric release).

## 2.2 Steam Sterilization — The Gold Standard

### 2.2.1 Principle

Steam sterilization (autoclaving) uses **saturated steam under pressure** to deliver thermal energy to microorganisms. The lethality does NOT come from the heat alone — it comes from the **latent heat of condensation**.

When steam contacts a cooler surface, it condenses into water, releasing **2,256 kJ/kg** of latent heat instantaneously. This causes:

1. **Protein denaturation** — irreversible coagulation of microbial enzymes
2. **Membrane disruption** — lipid bilayers melt
3. **DNA/RNA damage** — strand breaks due to heat

### 2.2.2 Why Steam Is Superior to Hot Air

| Property | Steam at 121°C | Dry Heat at 160°C |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Heat transfer medium | Condensing vapor (10× faster) | Convection (slow) |
| Penetration | Excellent (lumen-filling) | Poor |
| Cycle time | 15–30 min | 60–120 min |
| Damage to instruments | Minimal (if dried properly) | Causes oxidation |
| Spore kill time | ~3 min | ~30 min |

### 2.2.3 Standard Steam Sterilization Cycles (KTU Standard Reference)

| Cycle | Temperature | Pressure | Minimum Hold Time | Application |
|-------|-------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Gravity (slow) | 121°C | 103 kPa (15 psi) | **15 min** | Glassware, unwrapped instruments |
| Pre-vacuum (high) | 132–134°C | 207 kPa (30 psi) | **3–4 min** | Porous loads, wrapped instruments, linens |
| Flash (emergency) | 132°C | 207 kPa | **3 min** unwrapped / **10 min** wrapped | Emergency instrument turnaround in OT |
| Liquids | 121°C | 103 kPa | 20 min | Culture media, pharmaceutical solutions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does steam at 121°C kill everything?**
> At 121°C, the rate of protein denaturation in *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* (the reference biological indicator spore) increases by approximately **10⁸-fold** compared to 37°C. The spores literally cannot survive because their enzymes unfold.

### 2.2.4 The Steam Sterilization Cycle — Stage by Stage

1. **Loading** — Items packed to allow steam penetration (≤ 75% chamber volume).
2. **Air Removal** — Either by downward displacement (gravity) or vacuum pump (pre-vacuum).
3. **Heating & Conditioning** — Steam heats the load to target temperature.
4. **Sterilization (Hold/Exposure)** — Temperature held for the prescribed time.
5. **Drying** — Vacuum pulse + heat removes moisture (critical to prevent wet packs).
6. **Return to Atmospheric Pressure** — Sterile air filtered through HEPA (0.3 µm) enters.

## 2.3 Ethylene Oxide (EO) Sterilization

### 2.3.1 Why EO Is Used

Some materials **cannot** survive steam:
- Plastics (PVC, polyethylene, polystyrene)
- Electronics and fiber-optic cables
- Rubber, adhesives, certain polymers
- Pre-assembled devices with heat-sensitive components

EO is a **low-temperature (37–63°C)** sterilant that diffuses into packaging and instrument lumens, killing microbes by **alkylation** of proteins, DNA, and RNA.

### 2.3.2 Chemistry of EO Sterilization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkylation Reaction:** EO (C₂H₄O) attacks nucleophilic sites (-NH₂, -SH, -COOH, -OH) in microbial macromolecules, forming covalent bonds that destroy the molecule's biological function.

$$
\text{Protein} - \text{NH}_2 + \text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2 - \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Protein} - \text{NH} - \text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2 - \text{OH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **irreversible chemical modification**. The microbe cannot repair itself because its DNA and enzymes are covalently crosslinked.

### 2.3.3 EO Sterilization Cycle Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Range Permitted |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| EO Concentration | **600–1,200 mg/L** | 400–1,500 mg/L |
| Temperature | **37°C, 55°C, or 63°C** | 29–65°C |
| Relative Humidity | **40–80%** (optimum 60%) | 30–90% |
| Exposure Time | **1–6 hours** | 0.5–8 hours |
| Pressure | **−80 kPa (vacuum)** to +200 kPa | Variable |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Triad of EO Sterilization:** All three must be optimized simultaneously — **Gas concentration + Temperature + Humidity**. Reducing any one below the threshold causes failure even if the others are correct. This is a favorite KTU viva question.

### 2.3.4 EO Safety Considerations

- EO is **toxic, flammable, and a known carcinogen**.
- **Aeration phase** (mechanical or ambient) of **8–12 hours at 50–60°C** is mandatory to reduce residuals.
- OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) = **1 ppm (8-hr TWA)**.
- Hospital EO sterilizers are located in **dedicated, ventilated rooms** with continuous gas monitoring.

## 2.4 Filtration Sterilization

### 2.4.1 Principle

Fluids are passed through a membrane filter with pore sizes small enough to physically retain bacteria and spores. Used when heat or chemicals would damage the product (e.g., antibiotic solutions, vaccines, culture media with heat-sensitive ingredients, ophthalmic solutions).

### 2.4.2 Filter Ratings and Applications

| Pore Size | Retention Capability | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| **0.22 µm (0.2 µm)** | Bacterial removal (most common sterilization grade) | Sterile drug solutions |
| **0.45 µm** | Yeast and larger bacteria (clarification, not sterilization) | Pre-filtration step |
| **0.1 µm** | Mycoplasma removal | Cell culture media |
| **HEPA 0.3 µm** | 99.97% particle retention | Air filtration in OTs and LAFs |
| **ULPA 0.12 µm** | 99.999% particle retention | Cleanrooms (ISO Class 3–5) |

### 2.4.3 Types of Filters Used in Hospitals

- **Membrane filters** — Cellulose acetate, polyethersulfone (PES), PVDF
- **Depth filters** — Glass fiber, ceramic (for pre-filtration)
- **Cartridge filters** — Pleated membrane for high-volume flow
- **Syringe filters** — Inline, single-use for IV admixture

## 2.5 Autoclaves — Engineering Design and Operation

### 2.5.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **autoclave** is a pressurized steam sterilizer used in hospitals for reprocessing heat- and moisture-resistant medical devices. It consists of a pressure vessel (chamber), a steam generator, a vacuum system (in pre-vacuum models), a control system, and safety interlocks.

### 2.5.2 Types of Hospital Autoclaves

| Type | Cycle | Use Case |
|------|-------|----------|
| **Gravity displacement** | Air displaced by denser steam | Simple, robust, slower |
| **Pre-vacuum (high-speed)** | Mechanical air removal via vacuum pump | Porous loads, wrapped items |
| **Steam-flush pressure-pulse (SFPP)** | Repeated steam flush + pressure pulse | Energy-efficient alternative to vacuum |
| **B-class benchtop** | Vacuum + drying | Dental clinics, small CSSDs |
| **Large rectangular chamber** | High volume | Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD) |
| **Pass-through (double-door)** | Sterile side / unsterile side | Operating theatre corridor |

### 2.5.3 Components of a Modern Autoclave

1. **Chamber** — Double-walled stainless steel (typically SS 316L), ASME-certified pressure vessel
2. **Steam generator** — Dedicated electric or steam-fed boiler
3. **Vacuum pump** — Water ring or dry rotary vane (for pre-vacuum models)
4. **HEPA filter on air inlet** — 0.3 µm, prevents recontamination
5. **Control panel** — PLC-based, touch-screen HMI
6. **Safety valves** — Pressure relief, door interlock, temperature limit
7. **Drain system** — Steam trap, condensate coolers
8. **Recording devices** — Chart recorder, electronic batch log (21 CFR Part 11 compliant)

### 2.5.4 Autoclave Testing and Validation

- **Bowie-Dick test** — Daily, checks air removal efficiency (3.5 min at 134°C).
- **Helium leak test** — Annual, verifies chamber seal integrity.
- **Biological indicator test** — Weekly, *G. stearothermophilus* spore strips.
- **Chemical indicator (Class 1–6)** — Internal packs (Class 4–6) and external tape (Class 1).
- **Parametric release** — Computer-verified cycle parameters substitute for biological indicators in validated loads.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Concept | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---------|----------|---------|-------|
| **Steam pressure-temperature** | $P = P_{atm} \cdot e^{\left[\frac{L}{R_v} \left(\frac{1}{T_{boil}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron for steam saturation | Pa, K |
| **D-value (decimal reduction)** | $D = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_f)}$ | Time to kill 90% of population | min |
| **Z-value** | $Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2)}$ | Temperature change for 10× change in D | °C |
| **F-value (lethality)** | $F = \sum 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}} \cdot \Delta t$ | Equivalent time at reference temperature | min |
| **Steam dryness fraction** | $X = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{vapor} + m_{water}}$ | Quality of steam (≥ 0.95 per EN 285) | dimensionless |
| **Latent heat of condensation** | $Q = m \cdot L$ | Energy released when steam condenses (L = 2,256 kJ/kg) | kJ |
| **Log reduction** | $\log_{10}(N_0/N_f) = t/D$ | Sterilization efficacy | dimensionless |
| **EO concentration (mg/L)** | $C = \frac{m_{EO}}{V_{chamber}}$ | Dose of sterilant | mg/L |
| **Sterility Assurance Level** | $SAL = 10^{-6}$ | Probability of survival threshold | dimensionless |
| **Arrhenius kill rate** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ | Temperature dependence of microbial death | s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU board exam, memorize: **$D_{121} \approx 1.5$ min** for *G. stearothermophilus* and **$Z \approx 10°C$** for steam sterilization. These are the canonical values used in every textbook problem.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CSSD (Central Sterile Supply Department)** of a 500-bed hospital runs **12–20 autoclave cycles per day** to reprocess ~6,000–10,000 instrument sets monthly.
- **Pharmaceutical industries** use 0.22 µm filtration for **>2 billion doses of injectable drugs** per year globally.
- **EO sterilizers** are the only practical method for **>50% of disposable medical devices** (syringes, IV sets, catheters).
- **Steam economy** in modern autoclaves: a pre-vacuum cycle uses **~3 kg steam per 1 kg load** vs. 8 kg for gravity cycle — saving energy and water.
